title: Introduce kernel-level heatmap and cost-flow annotator for invocation traces
preview: Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to surface GPU/kernel-level queuing and contention hotspots correlated to token-level cost and per-stage latency. This PR adds a sampled, lightweight kernel-heatmap export and a cost-flow annotator that stitches stage timing with kernel occupancy to help SREs and runtime engineers diagnose expensive invocation patterns. Design summary: 1) A new kernel heatmap sampler emits compact histograms of kernel queue wait and execution time per-device and per-invocation-phase; sampling is prioritized for requests that exceed a configurable latency or cost threshold. 2) A cost-flow annotator postprocesses traces to attach a per-invocation-stage cost attribution vector (model load, key-value cache miss penalties, kernel stalls, token-generation time) and synthesizes rollup markers used by dashboards. 3) Minimal on-path overhead: sampler uses atomic ring-buffers and amortized aggregation at a local telemetry worker; annotation is done asynchronously by the trace aggregator to avoid adding tail latency. Implementation details: Added a new runtime probe (runtime/probes/kernel_sampler.cc) with export hooks to the observability pipeline, a proto schema (telemetry/protos/kernel_heatmap.proto) and a canonical generator for the annotator (observability/annotators/cost_flow_annotator.go). Also updated the console dashboards (dashboards/infra/kernel-heatmap-panel.json and dashboards/infra/cost-flow-explorer.json) and the SLO playbook (runbooks/kernel-contention.md). Rollout plan: feature gated behind observability.kernel_heatmap flag, disabled by default for existing customers. Canary enabled for internal clusters and a stage behind the rollout gate; follow-on step will enable 0.1% sampling for hosted tier and 1% for dedicated, then iterate. Backfill: included a small backfill job (observability/scripts/backfill_heatmap.py) to index recent traces for correlation testing. Testing: unit tests for sampler ring-buffer and proto serialization, integration test that validates annotations appear on traces end-to-end, and a perf microbenchmark showing <1.3% median p99 increase when sampling at 0.1% on benchmark lab. Migration/rollback: feature flag removal and a short migration script to drop the temporary heatmap index if disabled. Related: ENG-5421 (investigation into GPU contention during long prompts), ties to runbook playbooks introduced in previous telemetry work.

title: per-tenant throughput SLA enforcer + shared burst token refactor
preview:
Motivation: customers on Dedicated ask for predictable per-tenant throughput guarantees while still allowing short-term bursts across a shared fleet. This PR implements an in-runtime per-tenant throughput SLA enforcer plus a refactor of the fleet-wide shared burst token pool to support weighted allocation and backpressure coordination with the autoscaler.

Summary of changes: added a light-weight controller (runtime/fleet/sla_enforcer.go) that tracks per-tenant token budgets (tokens/sec) and allocates from the local host bucket first, then from the shared burst pool when available. The shared burst pool has been moved out of the legacy global dispatcher and into a refactored service (runtime/fleet/burst_pool.go) that exposes an async lease API and utilization metrics. The autoscaler now consumes burst-pool pressure signals to pre-warm capacity when pool exhaustion is sustained. Console and metrics surfaces updated to show per-tenant SLA usage and pool utilization. Documentation and runbook include guidance for setting tenant SLAs and fallback behavior.

Behavior and guarantees: tenants configured with a throughput_sla (rps/tokens-per-sec equivalent) will be rate-limited at the runtime layer; transient bursts up to configured burst_quota are served from the shared pool subject to per-tenant weight and global fairness. When the pool is exhausted for >2s for a tenant, the SLA enforcer starts returning a structured quota_exceeded error with an RFC-structured JSON body that downstream services can use for graceful degradation. Autoscaler integration: a new metric (burst_pool_pressure_ratio) is emitted and used by the Dedicated autoscaler to trigger proactive pre-warm scaling when sustained >75% for >30s.

Backward compatibility: all behaviors default to existing admission rules when no per-tenant SLA is configured. The shared pool refactor maintains previous heuristics but exposes knobs for weight and max_shares per tenant. Migration is guarded behind runtime feature flag "enable_sla_enforcer" (off by default in existing installs).

Testing: unit tests for lease accounting and weight arithmetic added; integration tests simulate multi-tenant burst scenarios and assert observability metrics and autoscaler hooks; e2e Canary harness included under perf-canary to validate latency/p99 under mixed workloads.

Related items: linked Linear tasks and runbook updates are referenced below. The PR is intentionally scoped to runtime and orchestration layers; model-serving kernels are unchanged.

Notes: this PR contains a couple of nontrivial refactors; reviewers, please pay attention to concurrency in burst_pool.go and to the metric tagging in runtime/telemetry.

title: Eviction Balance Controller and Sampled Utilization Inspector for Dedicated Fleets
preview: Context: In several recent incidents we observed oscillatory tenant evictions driven by noisy instantaneous utilization samples from the admission controller. Motivation: reduce user-facing throttles and preserve committed throughput while still reclaiming capacity when needed. This PR introduces two coordinated components: 1) Eviction Balance Controller (EBC) that applies a soft-decommit window with a configurable penalty curve and fairness drift limiter to spread eviction decisions across time and tenants, and 2) Sampled Utilization Inspector (SUI), a low-overhead agent-side sampler that feeds percentile-based utilization signals to the autoscaler instead of relying on single-sample probes. Key design points: EBC computes an eviction_score = (util_delta * penalty_multiplier(time_since_last_eviction) + backlog_factor) with a tunable sigmoid penalty curve to avoid immediate repeated evictions; it emits an intended_decommit set which the allocator reconciler gates through proportional backfill heuristics. SUI samples targets at 50ms intervals for a rolling 1m window and reports p50/p90/p99 to the headroom controller; sampling is probabilistic (per-core token bucket) to bound CPU and bandwidth. Compatibility: the controller implements an opt-in feature flag dedicated.eviction_balance_v2; when disabled the system falls back to legacy immediate-evict path. Observability: new metrics (dedicated/eviction_rate_smoothed, dedicated/eviction_intent_queue, dedicated/sample_util_p50/p90/p99, dedicated/eviction_penalty_curve) and traces for decision provenance. Testing: unit tests for penalty curve mapping and invariants, integration tests simulating churn and degraded headroom, load tests running on perf-lab with synthetic bursts showing a 37% reduction in user-visible throttles and 12% less tail-latency for reserved tenants. Rollout plan: 1) internal canary on perf-lab cluster, 2) staged rollout to two non-critical customer fleets with A/B routing for 48h, 3) gradual global roll to Dedicated fleets with 1% increments and automatic rollback on SLO regression. Risks and mitigations: mis-calibrated penalty curve could delay required evictions; mitigation includes safe-fail override that enforces hard-evict when headroom drops below absolute_threshold. Backwards compatibility: SUI falls back to legacy metrics on older agents.

title: Add compacted invocation digests and anomaly briefs
preview:
Context and motivation: We frequently see operator noise in long-running SLO investigations where individual invocation traces are large and repetitive. Responders spend time scrolling through kilobytes of token-level traces that differ only in a few fields. This PR introduces a compacted invocation digest format and a companion 'anomaly brief' summarizer to reduce noise, speed triage, and provide a stable summary for downstream alerting and runbooks.

What this changes: compacted invocation digests are a condensed trace representation persisted to our tracing backend for selected sampled invocations. Each digest encodes a small set of stable identifiers (callsite fingerprint, model_variant, tenant_id, prompt_shape_signature), coarse token-phase histograms (generation/encode/kv/memory), and a short anomaly vector (entropy_delta, cost_delta_pct, latency_tail_bucket). The anomaly brief is a human-readable one-line summary derived from the digest and stored alongside the trace in the log-index for fast search.

Goals: reduce mean investigation time for SRE/customer-facing incidents by >25%, lower long-term storage for sampled traces by ~40% for invocation digests, and enable downstream automated alert dampening using the anomaly brief score.

Non-goals: this PR does not change primary full-resolution trace retention or replace existing token-level cost accounting; it only introduces an auxiliary compact artifact to assist triage and dedup alerts.

Implementation notes:
- New package telemetry/digest with a compact on-wire format (v1) and JSON encoder for log-index ingestion.
- Sampling hook extension: sample decision now returns an optional digest payload for sampled invocations.
- Log-index writer: brief record emitted as a single log line with structure keys for search.
- Dashboard: added a small dashboard tile (Telemetry -> Investigations) showing digest counts, anomaly brief rates, and avg triage time.
- CLI/Docs: CLI tool to fetch and expand a compacted digest for debugging; docs describing fields and interpretation.

Backward compatibility: the compacted digest uses new keys and a version header — older readers ignore unknown headers. Rollout is safe on main because digest emission is gated behind a feature flag (telemetry.compacted_digest = off by default).

Checklist: 
- [x] Unit tests for digest encoder/decoder
- [x] Integration tests for sampler hook integration
- [x] E2E test that brief lands in log-index and is queryable
- [x] Dashboard tile + dashboard test snapshot
- [x] Short docs + CLI helper
- [x] Feature flag gating and roofline mem/cpu check

Commits summary:
- feat(telemetry): add compacted invocation digest data model and encoder
- feat(sampler): expose optional digest attachment from sample decision
- feat(logindex): write anomaly brief line on sampled invocations
- perf: use protobuf-lite for on-wire digest encoding
- test: add unit/integration tests and e2e for brief ingestion
- docs: describe compacted digest and how to expand via CLI

Changed files (high-level):
- telemetry/digest/encoder.go (new)
- telemetry/digest/decoder.go (new)
- telemetry/sampler/hook.go (modified)
- services/logindex/writer.go (modified)
- webapp/dashboards/telemetry_investigations.json (new)
- tools/cli/digest_inspect.go (new)
- docs/telemetry/compacted-digest.md (new)

Testing and CI: initial CI run hit a flaky test in logindex writer (race around buffer reuse), fixed by making writer allocate a temporary buffer per invocation in a follow-up commit. Full suite now passes on linux amd64 and the telemetry e2e job shows the brief landing in the test log-index. Load test: simulated 100k invocations with 0.5% digest sampling rate to validate CPU/memory overhead — additional CPU < 2% and memory < 8% for writer process.

Review notes and rollout plan: the feature flag allows staged rollout to telemetry clusters. Recommend enabling for staging first with a 0.1% sampling rate, monitor the digest_write_errors metric and digest_backpressure. After 48h with no regressions, increase to 0.5% in staging then enable for canary customer. Full production enable requires a safety signoff from SRE and Observability PM.

Impact on alerts: new anomaly_brief_count and anomaly_brief_rate metrics added. Existing alerts are unchanged but we will follow up with a small set of alert mutation PRs to leverage the brief score for deduping (separate PR).

References: Linear tickets ENG-3210 (digest design), ENG-3227 (dashboard tile), SUP-4121 (customer request to speed triage).

=== Review conversation (high level) ===
Ethan Park (reviewer): "Love the idea. Two asks: make the digest schema explicit in docs and ensure the brief score is explainable in runbooks."
Maya Chen (author): "Added schema section to docs and a short appendix to the runbook explaining how brief scores are derived."
Priya Nand (reviewer): "Can we confirm that the proto-lite encoding is compatible with the existing protobuf readers? Also request an extra unit test for malformed digest handling."
Maya Chen: "proto-lite is a subset designed to be forward compatible; added a unit test for corrupt/malformed bytes and defensive handling in decoder."
Diego Alvarez (reviewer): "Please verify writer resilience under backpressure. Also prefer limit to per-tenant digest emissions in initial rollout."
Maya Chen: "Added digest_backpressure metric and tenant-level config in sampler hook. Updated rollout notes to include per-tenant caps."

Merge/CI outcome: after 3 approvals and two minor fixes (buffer race, extra unit test), CI passed and PR was squash-merged on 2026-02-15.

Release notes (for changelog): "Observability: add compacted invocation digests and anomaly briefs to speed triage and reduce trace noise. Feature gated; see docs for enablement and interpretation."

title: surgical-load-sentinel-and-transparent-readonly-fallback
preview: Context: We observed a pattern of transient KV backpressure spikes causing cascading retries and SLO violations during short regional pressure events. This change introduces a surgical load sentinel that detects short-lived KV pressure and routes affected requests to a transparent readonly fallback when safe. Motivation: Preserve tail latency and reduce amplified error/latency cascades without wholesale throttling of tenants. Keep changes conservative to avoid behavioral surprises. Design summary: 1) Load sentinel in controlplane monitors kvPressure, inflightPerTenant and recent error-rate histograms and computes a lightweight score. 2) When score exceeds a configured short window threshold the sentinel marks traffic for an incremental readonly fallback using a routing hint. 3) The readonly fallback path in route-mux preserves identity headers and KV snapshot tokens and returns best-effort read-only responses; write requests receive a soft-deny with clear error code to clients. 4) Circuit cooldown and penalty decay prevent flapping. 5) Feature gated and rollouts via sentinel-config with conservative defaults. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests for sentinel heuristics and cooldown - [x] Integration tests for fallback routing and trace propagation - [x] Metrics and alerting for sentinel trips - [x] Runbook drafted and linked - [x] Staged rollout config included Commits summary: a1f4b8c initial sentinel prototype and tests; b2d9e3f integrate readonly fallback path into route-mux and preserve trace headers; c3f7a11 add metrics, alerts and runbook entries; d4e2b20 CI checks and threshold tweaks. Files changed (high level): services/controlplane/src/sentinel/load_sentinel.rs added core detection logic, comments, and Prometheus hooks; route-mux fallback path updates add readonly routing and header passthrough; runbook and staging k8s overlay for gated rollout; CI and console flag. Small snippet: `if kv_pressure.window(3s) > threshold && inflight_per_tenant > tenant_cap {mark_readonly_hint(tx)}` Review thread: Ethan Park: "Nice focused approach. Two asks: expose the cooldown as a config and add an e2e that simulates KV spike to assert no double-counting across tenants." Sonia Patel (author): "Added cooldown config and unit tests that exercise decay; e2e simulated in staging overlay, see staging config. Addressed double-counting by scoping metrics to tenant-id." Maya Singh: "We should be explicit about error codes for writes when in readonly fallback; will this surface to SDKs?" Sonia Patel: "Writes return a 409 with `x-redwood-fallback=readonly`; SDKs will map to a Retryable=false error; console flag documents UX." Julien Moreau: "Metrics look good, consider a low-cardinality 'sentinel_reason' tag for easier rollups." Sonia Patel: "Added sentinel_reason tag with values 'kv_pressure'|'inflight'|'err_rate' and tests updated." CI notes: All pipeline stages passed after second run. Unit tests 142/142, integration smoke passed, lint warnings fixed in second commit. Performance bench: synthetic kv spike test in benchmark-lab shows 28% reduction in 99p tail latency for impacted tenants with <3% cost overhead. Merge notes: Squash-merged into main after approvals from Ethan and Maya. Deployed to staging with feature-flag off for production; gradual rollout plan in runbook. Post-merge actions: monitor sentinel.trip_rate, sentinel.duration_p95, and readonly-fallback.rate for 48h; be ready to revert via feature flag.

title: enable structured ci telemetry and lightweight presubmit profiles
preview:
Motivation: presubmit runs have become a major contributor to CI latency and noisy alerts. We need two coordinated improvements: (1) standardized, structured telemetry across CI lanes so presubmit and merge pipelines emit consistent metrics; (2) a lightweight presubmit profile that runs fast, language-aware smoke checks on small diffs to speed feedback for engineers. This PR introduces a small telemetry bridge (OTLP -> Prometheus) and a new presubmit profile driver that selects minimal checks via a delta-based matcher.

What I changed (high level): condensed CI telemetry into a single structured schema (ci.build.*, ci.test.*, ci.job.duration_ms) and a small exporter in ci/telemetry that translates workflow annotations to OTLP and Prometheus-compatible metrics; refactored .github/workflows/presubmit.yml to add a new presubmit:light job that uses the delta router to select a compact set of linters/tests; moved heavyweight integration tests to presubmit:long and nightly lanes; added a manual dep-bisector workflow scripts/dep-bisect.sh that can be triggered from failing presubmit runs to speed triage of dependency regressions. Also updated docs and onboarding notes (docs/ci/telemetry.md) and added a compatibility shim so older dashboards (legacy_ci_*) continue to receive basic counters for a transition period.

Checklist: - [x] Add structured telemetry bridge and tests - [x] Implement delta-based presubmit router - [x] Add presubmit:light job and small plugin adapter for language profiles - [x] Update docs and migration notes - [x] Smoke-test on staging CI and iterate on flaky rules

Commits summary: - initial: add ci/telemetry exporter and schema definition - refactor: presubmit router + delta matcher - feature: presubmit:light workflow and plugin adapter - ops: add dep-bisect workflow + helper script - docs: docs/ci/telemetry.md + migration guide

Files touched (high level): .github/workflows/presubmit.yml, .github/workflows/dep-bisect.yml, ci/telemetry/exporter.go, ci/telemetry/schema.md, tools/lint-plugin/adapter.ts, scripts/dep-bisect.sh, docs/ci/telemetry.md, docs/ci/presubmit-migration.md, tests/ci/telemetry_test.go, charts/ci-metrics/values.yaml, Makefile (ci targets).

Why this approach: structured telemetry lets SREs write one set of alerting and dashboards across all CI lanes and enables better correlation between presubmit failures and flakes. The lightweight presubmit profile preserves safety (fast smoke checks on changed files) while offloading slow suites to explicitly named lanes. We also needed a triage path for dependency-related regressions; dep-bisect is non-blocking and can be invoked from build annotations.

Testing and rollout: staged rollout: (1) enable telemetry bridge in staging (observed metric parity with legacy counters), (2) enable presubmit:light for a sample set of repos for two weeks, (3) widen to org default and adjust gate thresholds. Ran the new profile against a snapshot of 2k recent PRs to validate that the presubmit:light reduces CPU and test-runtime by ~48% on average for small diffs. CI: initial run saw one flaky lint rule in the TypeScript adapter which was fixed in a follow-up commit; final green on all checks.

Review notes / tradeoffs: this refactor centralizes telemetry collection which adds a small Go binary (exporter) to the CI image; it increases image size by ~12MB but the runtime overhead is negligible. The presubmit router relies on delta detection heuristics — we included an allowlist for critical paths (e.g., infra/*, api-schema/*) to force full presubmit when necessary. We also included an opt-out label (ci/full-presubmit) for teams who want to preserve previous behavior during migration.

Review conversation (high-level): Omar: "Can we avoid adding another long-running binary to job containers?" — Reply: "exporter runs briefly at job start/end and is built into the hermetic ci image; we measured <30ms overhead per job." Priya: "Delta heuristics look good but we should add an explicit safety net for infra changes." — Reply: "Added infra allowlist and a test to validate detection." Ethan: "Dep-bisect script is powerful — document RBAC and rate limits." — Reply: "Added docs/ci/dep-bisect.md and guard that requires workflow_dispatch and a small token scope."

Merge outcome: merged by squash-merge after two approvals and green CI. Migration follow-up tickets created for dashboard updates and a short deprecation window for legacy counters.

title: Add incident taxonomy dashboards + alerting (type/owner breakdowns + playbook links)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds the initial Incident Taxonomy (v1) dashboards + a small set of alert rules, per the reliability program initiative. The goal is to make taxonomy classification measurable and to enable reporting/triage by incident_type + owner_team without exploding metric label cardinality.

Key deliverables in this PR:
- Grafana dashboard: **Incident Taxonomy (v1)**
  - incident volume and impact proxy panels (by tier, severity)
  - breakdowns by **incident_type** and **owner_team**
  - classification compliance (% unknown/unclassified)
  - MTTA/MTTR approximations using incident event timestamps
  - drilldowns from type -> owner -> recent incidents (table)
  - playbook links based on incident_type (panel links + table column)
- Prometheus rules:
  - recording rules to normalize/aggregate incident event counters into a stable set of labels
  - alert rules for (1) unclassified rate regression (2) spike of incidents by type/owner (3) sustained active incidents for Dedicated/Private

## Context / Motivation
As part of the Reliability Program (“Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”), incident_type/subtype and owner are now required fields in incident tooling. We need dashboards/alerts that:
- show whether classification is being used correctly (and where it’s not)
- create a feedback loop for top recurring incident categories
- allow on-call to quickly jump from “what’s happening” -> “who owns it” -> “what runbook should I follow”

This PR targets the spec in Confluence (Incident taxonomy dashboards spec) and ties into Linear ENG-4828.

## Design notes (cardinality + data sources)
Data sources used:
- `incident_events_total{event=...}` emitted from incident-bot pipeline / annotations ingestion
- existing `request_errors_total` and `request_latency_seconds` are NOT relabeled here; the dashboard focuses on incidents as the unit of analysis

Cardinality constraints:
- `incident_type` is treated as a strict enum (v1 allowlist)
- `owner_team` is constrained to a small allowlist of on-call teams (e.g., SRE, Fleet, Routing, Runtime, Observability, SecurityOps, PrivateDeploy)
- `incident_subtype` is intentionally not used as a Prometheus label in this PR (shown only in the incident table sourced from annotations/events)
- `unknown` is a first-class bucket to avoid missing data (explicitly tracked)

## What changed
### Dashboards
- New dashboard: `dashboards/incident-taxonomy-v1.jsonnet`
  - Variables: `tier`, `severity`, `owner_team`, `incident_type`, `env`, `region`
  - Panels:
    - “Incidents created (last 24h/7d) by incident_type”
    - “Incidents created by owner_team”
    - “Active incidents over time” (stacked by tier)
    - “Classification compliance” (% known vs unknown)
    - “Top incident types (28d) + median time-to-mitigate”
    - “Recent incidents” table (links to Slack channel + incident doc + runbook)
  - Playbook links:
    - Uses a small mapping file to link type -> runbook URL (SRE/Infra/Runtime runbooks)

### Prometheus rules
- `prometheus/rules/incident_taxonomy_recording_rules.yaml`
  - Normalizes `incident_events_total` into:
    - `ri:incidents_created:count{tier,severity,incident_type,owner_team}`
    - `ri:incidents_active:count{tier,severity,incident_type,owner_team}`
    - `ri:incidents_unclassified:count{tier,severity}`
- `prometheus/alerts/incident_taxonomy_alerts.yaml`
  - `IncidentTaxonomyUnclassifiedRateHigh` (warn): unclassified >= 10% over 6h (all tiers)
  - `IncidentSpikeByTypeOwner` (warn): 3x baseline over 2h for any (incident_type, owner_team) in Hosted
  - `SustainedActiveIncidentsDedicatedPrivate` (page): active incidents >= 2 for 30m for Dedicated/Private

## Example queries (for reviewers)
- Incidents created by type:
  - `sum by (incident_type) (increase(ri:incidents_created:count{tier="$tier"}[24h]))`
- Unclassified rate:
  - `sum(increase(ri:incidents_unclassified:count{tier="$tier"}[6h])) / sum(increase(ri:incidents_created:count{tier="$tier"}[6h]))`

## Screenshots
(Attached in PR UI)
- Incident Taxonomy (v1) overview
- Drilldown: Routing-owned incidents, last 14d
- Classification compliance panel with unknown bucket

## Rollout / Ops
- Safe to deploy behind existing dashboard folder permissions.
- Alert routing:
  - warn alerts to `#eng-sre` (triage)
  - page alerts to `#incidents` + paging policy for SRE primary (owner_team is informational; paging by owner will be part of ENG-4823)

## Testing
- `make lint`
- `make dashboards-test` (jsonnet render)
- `promtool test rules`
- Verified panels render in staging Grafana (sre-observability)

## Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Reduce/lock down any remaining label surfaces if we see unexpected churn in `owner_team` values (see review notes).
- Add saved views per tier/team once CS permissions model is finalized.
- Add explicit MTTR SLA breach overlays once remediation SLA timers land (ENG-4829).

---
Closes: ENG-4828

title: Stratified burst budget distributor and tiered degradation plan for Dedicated fleet
preview:
Motivation: Tenants on Dedicated fleets need more predictable degradation and fairer use of limited burst credits during transient demand spikes. Current global soft-burst pools provide throughput but cause noisy neighbor effects and opaque degradation patterns. This change introduces a stratified burst budget distributor and a tiered degradation plan to give operators fine-grained control over how burst capacity is allocated and how tenants degrade when capacity is constrained.

What this PR does: Implements a new budget distributor that divides burst credits into named strata (critical, standard, opportunistic) and allocates credits to tenants based on configurable weights, recent utilization percentiles, and tenant commitment tiers. Adds an anticipatory refill algorithm (EWMA-based arrival estimate + percentile headroom multiplier) so short-lived surges are smoothed without inflating steady-state consumption.

Key features: 1) Config-driven strata: fleet operators can define strata names, target percentile thresholds, and per-strata reclaim priorities. 2) Tenant scoring: runtime computes a transient score per tenant combining weight, p50/p95 utilisation deltas, and recent burst consumption to determine which strata they draw from. 3) Tiered degradation plan: when fleet-wide capacity approaches configured limits, the plan enacts gradual degradation by stepping tenants down from opportunistic -> standard -> critical; critical tenants retain minimum headroom guarantees. 4) Safety and rollout: change is gated behind feature flag tenant_strata_rollout (off by default). Existing behavior is preserved unless strata are configured. 5) Telemetry: new metrics and aggregated reports to observe per-strata balances and tenant degradation events.

Implementation notes: - New core components: pkg/autoscaler/stratified_distributor.go, pkg/autoscaler/strata_policy.go - Extensions to DedicatedCapacitySpec (api/v1beta1/dedicated.proto) with clear validation rules and defaults. - Telemetry instrumentation added in telemetry/metrics.go and observability/alerts.yaml to surface regression in p95 latency when degradation steps occur. - Unit + integration tests cover allocation invariants, fallback to legacy global pool, and degradation step ordering. - A perf-canary job (benchmark-lab/canaries/strata-10k-tenants.yaml) demonstrates behavior under mixed commitment cohorts.

Backward compatibility and migration: The DedicatedCapacitySpec changes are additive. If burstStrata is not set, the distributor falls back to the legacy single-pool allocator. All proto changes are optional fields and include server-side defaults. The rollout plan documented in docs/dedicated/strata-rollout.md prescribes a staged enablement: internal canary -> opt-in customers -> graduated default.

Operational considerations: - New metrics: redwood_strata_credit_balance{strata}, redwood_tenant_burst_balance{tenant}, redwood_strata_eviction_events{strata} - Alerting: add SLO guard for rapid p95 regressions after enabling strata on a fleet (see observability/alerts.yaml) - Suggested knob values and a sample policy are included in examples/strata-sample-policy.yaml

Related work: builds on prior autoscaler telemetry plumbing (ENG-2310) and observer-based reservation contracts (ENG-1823).

Testing: comprehensive unit tests added (pkg/autoscaler/*_test.go), integration tests in test/integration/strata_integration_test.go, and a load canary in benchmark-lab. CI initially hit a flaky integration test exercising time.Sleep-based refill; I replaced the time dependency with a controllable clock in the distributor and re-ran CI.

Merge notes: squash-merge after sign-off from runtime and SRE.

title: Introduce lightweight 'skillshot' CI lane and dep-lock gardener for devdep hygiene
preview:
Context and motivation: Developers reported slow feedback for small iterative changes (15-25s setup + 3-7m test runs) which discourages frequent commits. This PR introduces a low-latency presubmit lane called 'skillshot' that exercises a narrow, language-aware subset of linters and smoke-tests to give sub-60s signal for common quick changes. In parallel, it adds a 'dep-lock gardener' utility that detects stale devDependencies, suggests removals, and maintains a small curated allowlist for dev tooling. This is aimed at reducing CI noise, minimizing install time, and lowering presubmit flakiness.

What changed (high level):
- Add new GitHub Actions workflow .github/workflows/ci-skillshot.yml that runs a trimmed toolchain (node install cache, python venv warm, quick lint + unit smoke) and returns status quickly.
- Introduce a modular presubmit-adapter pattern in infra/ci/adapters/ to plugin per-language "skillshot" checks. Adapters are small Go + shell shims that compute which checks are relevant based on changed file globs.
- Add tools/dep-lock-gardener/ which contains a lightweight CLI that analyzes package.json/pyproject.toml/dev-requirements and emits a candidate removal list; a separate GH Action job runs it nightly and opens suggested cleanup PRs (opt-in).
- Update README/ci.md with developer workflow and how to opt into the skillshot lane during draft PRs.

Commits (summary):
- 6f2b3a1  Add ci-skillshot workflow and docs (Maya Chen)
- 1a7d8c4  Implement presubmit adapter interface + node/python adapters (Maya Chen)
- fa4b202  Add dep-lock-gardener CLI and nightly workflow (Maya Chen)
- 9c3e5f0  Fix lint pathing and caching for skillshot runner (address reviewer feedback)

Files touched (representative):
- .github/workflows/ci-skillshot.yml  (new)
- .github/workflows/dep-lock-gardener-nightly.yml  (new)
- infra/ci/adapters/adapter.go  (new)
- infra/ci/adapters/node_adapter.sh  (new)
- infra/ci/adapters/python_adapter.sh  (new)
- tools/dep-lock-gardener/main.go  (new)
- README/ci.md  (updated)
- scripts/ci/cache-warm.sh  (updated)

Small diff snippets (illustrative):
- .github/workflows/ci-skillshot.yml:
  jobs:
    skillshot:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        - name: Warm caches -> uses: actions/cache@v4
        - name: Run skillshot -> run: ./infra/ci/run_skillshot.sh

- infra/ci/adapters/adapter.go (interface):
  package adapters
  type Adapter interface {
    Matches(files []string) bool
    RunSkillshot() error
  }

Review conversation (high level and timeline):
- Marco Diaz (reviewer): "Nice approach. Can we make the adapter interface language-agnostic (not node/python specific) and ensure adapters are hermetic? Also worried about duplicate nightly PRs from the gardener."
- Maya (author): "Agreed on hermeticity—adapters run in sandboxed containers and only use cached runtime images. Gardener creates PRs with a label `dep-gardener` and a fingerprint so duplicates are suppressed. Added tests for both behaviors."
- Priya Nair: "Please add opt-out config for repos with pinned workflows and ensure the skillshot doesn't prevent full presubmit from running."
- Maya: "Added `skillshot.enabled` flag in repo-level ci config and made skillshot optional. Full presubmit remains the default merge gate."
- Ethan Park: "There was a lint failure on the first run due to path changes in infra/ci; after fixing path normalization and cache keys we got green CI."
- Marco: approved with comment: "Consider telemetry (skillshot hit/miss) for next iteration."

CI run history: initial run (2026-01-28) -> fail (golangci lint path errors), second run after fix (2026-01-30) -> partial pass (skillshot passed, gardener unit test flaky), patched tests and reran (2026-02-02) -> all checks passed. Nightly action simulated locally during review.

Operational notes and rollout:
- Skillshot is enabled opt-in by default for feature branches. README/ci.md contains a one-liner to opt-in: `ci: skillshot: true` in .redwood-ci.yml. We plan a staged rollout across teams (developer tools, sdk, docs) before enabling widely.
- Dep-lock gardener runs nightly on a cron; suggested PRs are labeled `dep-gardener` and require a maintainer to review before merge. It does not auto-merge currently.

Compatibility and risk assessment:

- Add tools/dep-lock-gardener/ which contains a lightweight CLI that analyzes package.json/pyproject.toml/dev-requirements and emits a candidate removal list; a separate GH Action job runs it nightly and opens suggested cleanup PRs (opt-in).

title: Packed INT8 microkernel rewrite and token coalescer to improve throughput for small-batch inference
preview:
Context and motivation: small-batch interactive workloads (chat, per-request short-context generation) show poor INT8 utilization because microkernel packing and per-token scheduling produce many narrow GEMM shapes and high per-call overhead. Prior batching heuristics mitigated this at the request layer but left inefficiencies inside the kernel and scheduling layer. This PR introduces two coordinated changes to address that: (1) a packed INT8 microkernel optimized for narrow micro-tiles and reduced memory traffic, and (2) a runtime token coalescer that merges short token sequences across concurrent requests into aligned micro-batches at the kernel boundary.

Design summary:
- Packed INT8 microkernel: new micro-tiling layout (packed 4x8 major order) that reduces memory stride and enables vector-friendly inner loops; fused requantization step merged into the GEMM write-back to avoid a full dequant/q-requant pass for common patterns; safe FP32/FP8 fallback path for layers where requant scales exceed per-tensor range.
- Token coalescer scheduler: lightweight aggregator in the continuous batching pipeline that groups tokens by model variant and sequence length bucket, enforcing alignment to the microkernel tile size. Coalescer is conservative: it only aggregates tokens within configurable latency budget and respects per-tenant isolation.
- Runtime controls and telemetry: feature gates runtime.packed_int8.enabled and runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms; new metrics token_coalescer.groups, microkernel_tile_util, packed_int8_path_ratio.

Commits (high-level):
- feat(kernels): add packed int8 microkernel entrypoints and assembly fallback (3 commits)
- perf(scheduler): token coalescer hook into continuous batching loop (2 commits)
- test: unit + fuzz parity tests for INT8 path (2 commits)
- bench: add small-batch harness + CI microbench (1 commit)
- docs: runtime flags and rollout notes (1 commit)

Files touched (high level):
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.cc
- src/runtime/kernels/packed_int8_kernel.h
- asm/packed_int8_asm.S (platform-guarded implementations)
- src/runtime/scheduler/token_coalescer.cc
- src/runtime/scheduler/continuous_batching_integration.cc
- tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc
- benchmarks/bench_small_batch_packed_int8.py
- docs/runtime/flags.md
- examples/packed_int8_quickstart.md
- ci/microbench.yml
- build/BUILD.bazel (kernel entries)
- scripts/perf/compare_a100_t4.sh

Implementation notes:
- Microkernel implements a fused write-back that applies the per-channel scale and zero point during the GEMM output path; this removed one intermediate tensor allocation in the hot loop.
- We chose a 4x8 tile because it balances register pressure and memory coalescing on both A100 and T4; hand-tuned inner loops for AVX3 and CUDA mma-style intrinsics where available.
- Token coalescer hooks before the KV-cache dispatch: it accumulates tokens by (model_id, variant, seq_bucket) and emits coalesced batches up to the microkernel tile alignment or latency budget.

Correctness and parity:
- Added deterministic parity tests that compare outputs vs FP32 reference on representative micro-batches and randomized seeds.
- FP8 fallback uses an extra path that quantizes to FP8 when per-tensor scale fits; otherwise falls back to per-channel INT8 with a narrow requant path to preserve numerical fidelity.

Benchmarks (representative, run on on-prem clusters):
- A100 (8x latency-bound small queries): baseline INT8 path throughput 1250 tokens/s -> packed-int8 + coalescer 1650 tokens/s (+32%).
- T4 (mixed small-batch): baseline 740 -> 915 tokens/s (+23%).
- Median end-to-end p95 latency change: -4% (slightly improved due to fewer memory copies).
- Token-coalescer shows larger wins for <8-token requests; negligible impact for large batches.

Review conversation (selected excerpts, chronological):
- Ethan Park (review): "Can you add a microbench in CI that asserts no regression in token-level throughput on T4? Also needs a non-regression accuracy test (FP32 vs INT8) for 2M random tokens."
- Author (Monica): "Added microbench.yml and parity test; microbench is gated on an opt-in perf job to avoid CI costs. See tests/unit/test_packed_int8_parity.cc."
- Sofia Ruiz (review): "Concern: coalescer may increase tail latency for single-tenant extremes. We should expose latency_ms threshold and default to 5ms; also add tenant opt-out."
- Author: "Exposed runtime.token_coalescer.latency_ms with default 5, and runtime.token_coalescer.enable_per_tenant=false; added docs. Token coalescer will never coalesce across tenant boundaries by default."
- Jasper Li (review): "Assembly fallback looks good, but please add a safety check for AVX availability and a clean fallback to scalar path."
- Author: "Implemented CPU capability checks and unit tests for the scalar fallback path."
- CI pipeline: initial run failed due to clang-format on packed_int8_kernel.h and a flaky perf test; lint fixes and re-run produced green CI: unit tests 245/245 passed; microbench opt-in run showed throughput numbers above."

Rollout and rollback plan:
- Feature gates available via runtime flags; rollout to 10% of hosted traffic for first week with telemetry checks.
- Automated regression alerts on model quality (per-prompt edit distance) and token-level checksum parity will trigger immediate rollback.
- If severe regressions occur, we can disable runtime.packed_int8.enabled remotely and revert scheduler hook.

Risk assessment:
- Numerical drift: parity tests and FP8 fallback minimize risk; however, rare edge cases may see small token-level differences—these are surfaced to evals automatically.
- Multi-tenant fairness: coalescer respects tenant isolation; added opt-out flag for customers that prefer per-request isolation.

Follow-ups and TODO:
- Explore widening tile shapes for large-batch paths and auto-tune tile size per-GPU.
- Add cooperative scheduling with dedicated capacity autoscaler to avoid starvation when coalescer groups many small requests.
- Port microkernel to the Windows CUDA toolchain (currently Linux-only guarded).

Merge notes:
- PR was squashed on merge.
- Merged by Ethan Park after approvals and green CI.
- Associated Linear cards closed: ENG-8421, ENG-8484.
- Release: rollout will be gated and documented under v0.67 runtime release notes.

title: Drift‑resilient collective scheduler and KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention
preview:
Context and motivation: In production we continue to see sporadic tail latency spikes on multi‑GPU TP workloads correlated with short windows of NCCL congestion and many small KV cache allocations from continuous batching. This PR introduces two coordinated runtime changes: (1) a drift‑resilient collective scheduler that perturbs and staggers NCCL launches to avoid pathological synchronization across tensor slices, and (2) a KV slab consolidation pass that reduces small allocation churn and improves L2/L3 locality for the KV cache allocator. The combined change targets multi‑GPU tensor‑parallel inference paths (TP runtime) and the continuous‑batching code path used by the API router.

What this PR does (high level): - Adds Scheduler::PerturbationWindow, a lightweight jittering layer that applies per‑peer launch offsets within a configurable window to break lockstep NCCL launches while preserving ordering and progress guarantees. The scheduler also tracks per‑peer outstanding credits and backs off launches when cumulative outstanding bytes exceed a runtime threshold. - Adds KVSlab::Consolidate and a consolidation task that merges hot small slabs into larger contiguous slabs at low priority, reducing per‑token allocation overhead and fragmentation. Consolidation is gated by memory pressure and configurable thresholds so it won’t interfere with SLOs. - Adds a small admission tweak to continuous batching: older requests receive a priority boost in the batch selector to reduce head‑of‑line latency under high QPS. - Benchmarks and regression tests: microbenchmarks for NCCL launch variance, end‑to‑end tp inference perf harness, and an integration test that exercises slab consolidation under load.

Design notes and rationale: - We intentionally avoid deterministic reordering. The perturbation window is randomized per launch but bounded; this prevents pathological worst‑case alignments while keeping collectives efficient. - Consolidation is incremental and infrequent; it uses a background worker with a budgeted runtime slice to avoid interfering with token service. - The NCCL perturbation is conservative: on rigs where we observe no contention, the perturbation window defaults to zero. Operators can tune the window via runtime flags and per‑cluster policies. - The combined effect reduces contention-caused retries and kernel stalls while also lowering memory allocation overhead that exacerbates NVLink traffic.

Commits (summary): - 1a2f3b4: Introduce perturbation scheduler hooks and config knobs; add unit tests for scheduling invariants. - 2b3c4d5: Implement KV slab consolidation pass, background worker, and new allocator stats. - 3c4d5e6: Integrate with continuous batching admission, add perf test harness and perf metrics, update docs and changelog.

Files changed (high level): - runtime/scheduler/perturbation_scheduler.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/kv_slab_consolidator.{cc,h} - runtime/kv_cache/allocator_stats.{cc,h} - runtime/batching/batch_selector.{cc} (priority tweak) - tests/perf/tp_nccl_stress_test.py - ci/perf/benchmarks/launch_variance.yaml - docs/release_notes/2026-02-03-kv-nccl-optimization.md - examples/perf/tuning/README.md

Tiny illustrative snippet (informational only): "Scheduler::PerturbationWindow" accepts a window_ns and seed; when >0 it computes offset = rng.uniform(0, window_ns) and schedules NCCL launch at planned_time + offset. KVSlab::Consolidate scans slab list for contiguous small slabs with same owner and merges them under lock.

Review conversation (highlights): - Ethan Park (reviewer): "Nice isolation of perturbation logic. A few requests: make the default window configurable via env var, and add a per‑node debug metric for perturbed launches." Author reply: "Added RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS env and metric runtime.scheduler.perturbed_launches_count; updated tests." - Lucia Ramirez: "Concern about consolidation causing a stop‑the‑world when merging many small slabs." Author reply: "Consolidation uses a background budgeted worker; added unit test and a runtime flag to disable consolidation during low latency SLO periods." - Owen Patel: "Can we get a perf comparison on a 8xA100 TP run?" Author reply: "Included 8x A100 numbers below; will add CI perf job once this merges." - Followups: minor doc edits, one flaky test disabled and a TODO added to re-enable after allocator lock refactor.

CI summary: - Linux unit tests: pass - Integration perf harness (short): pass - Lint: pass - Extended perf run (internal): nominal but required one rerun due to infra transient. Overall status: pass.

Benchmarks (selected results, end‑to‑end TP inference harness, 8x A100, mixed sequence lengths): - Baseline (main): median throughput = 1000 infer/s, p99 latency = 210ms, peak NVLink utilization = 78% - This PR: median throughput = 1140 infer/s (+14%), p99 latency = 185ms (-11.9%), peak NVLink utilization = 66% (-15.4%) - KV memory fragmentation (tracked via allocator_stats.hot_slab_frag): baseline 0.18, this PR 0.09 (-50%) Notes: numbers averaged across 20 runs with warm‑up. Workloads with many small prefixes saw larger gains.

Backward compatibility and operator impact: - Defaults keep behavior unchanged (window defaults to 0, consolidation disabled under heavy SLO by default). - Operators can enable the window and consolidation via flags or cluster policy in the console. - Added new runtime metrics and two flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS and RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED documented in docs/release_notes.

Follow‑ups and TODOs: - Add an operator dashboard tile to surface perturbed launch counts and consolidation activity. - Extend consolidation heuristics to account for pinned memory slabs. - Add a canary rollout in Dedicated environments to validate across different network topologies.

Release notes (short): "Introduce a drift‑resilient collective scheduler and background KV slab consolidation to reduce NCCL contention and small allocation overhead; typical throughput gains 10–15% on multi‑GPU TP workloads and reduced p99 latency. Flags: RW_SCHED_PERTURB_NS, RW_KV_CONSOLIDATE_ENABLED."

Related: ENG-1284 (collective tail latency task), ENG-1301 (kv allocator fragmentation).

title: introduce-policy-contracts-kms-commitment-ledger-and-residency-gate
preview: Context and motivation: Customers operating under jurisdictional controls asked for stronger, auditable guarantees that (1) requests that claim a region-bound residency are actually served with region-anchored CMKs, (2) operator principles cannot bypass tenant-scoped RBAC policies, and (3) audit trails contain verifiable cryptographic markers tying requests to KMS commitments. This PR implements three coordinated pieces: (A) a lightweight Policy Contracts DSL and compiler that lets product and compliance own high-level contracts (e.g., "Tenant must use CMK in eu-west-1 for classification=restricted") and compiles into RBACv2 assertions and enforcer rules; (B) a KMS Commitment Ledger service that records authoritative, append-only KMS assignment decisions per request (signed by the enforcer) and surfaces a compact proof token that is attached to audit events; (C) a Residency Gate enforcement shim that runs in the mux layer to block/redirect requests that violate region pin constraints and surface clear error codes for callers. Implementation summary: - New package rbac-policy-bundle/policy_contracts: DSL spec (YAML first-class), compiler, and unit tests; includes example contracts for tenant residency, operator-scoped denylists, and service-level allowances. - Enforcer integration: authz/enforcer now consumes compiled policy contracts at startup (and supports runtime refresh). When the enforcer binds a KMS choice to a request it issues a KMS Commitment entry (ledger client) and returns a compact commitment token (base64url) which is attached to the request context. - Commitment ledger: services/commitment-ledger introduces a small append-only service with a signing key (rotatable via KMS). Entries include request_id, chosen_kms_key_id, region, policy_contract_id, signer_kid, and signature. Ledger exposes a read-only API for auditors and an expiry-based retention policy configured per-tenant. - Audit enrichment: audit/forwarder picks up commitment tokens and expands them into commitment metadata (last 8 chars of signer_kid, region, contract id hash) while preserving the original event. Enriched audit records pass through existing pipelines unchanged for consumers that don't need the extra fields. - Residency Gate: ingress/mux/residency_gate implements a fast path check for region assertions and KMS compatibility matrix. Failing checks return 403 with an explicit policy_violation code and a link to remediation docs. - Migration & rollout: feature flag rbac_policy_contracts.enabled with staged rollout; sticky migration script migrates a small cohort of tenants to the new enforcement path; compatibility shim ensures legacy bindings still resolve to prior KMS routing if the tenant is not migrated. Commits (high-level): - add(policy_contracts): DSL + compiler + examples - feat(enforcer): runtime contract loader + ledger client - feat(ledger): append-only commitment store + signer integration - feat(audit): enrich forwarder with commitment tokens - feat(mux): residency gate enforcement + error codes - docs: operator migration guide + consumer audit docs - tests: unit + integration + one flake-fix commit Tests and CI: - Unit test coverage added for DSL compiler, ledger signing and verification, and residency gate logic. - Integration tests include a simulated tenant migration with an in-memory ledger. - CI matrix: linux/amd64 unit, integration:e2e, lint, go-sec-scan. One flaky test (audit/forwarder TestBatchFlush) was fixed by replacing time.Sleep with a condition variable; CI green on rerun. Files changed (high-level): - rbac-policy-bundle/policy_contracts/{spec.md,schema.yaml,compiler.go,compiler_test.go,examples/tenant_residency.yaml} - authz/enforcer/{loader.go,binding_resolver.go,ledger_client.go} - services/commitment-ledger/{server.go,store.go,signer.go,api.md} - audit/forwarder/{enricher.go,enricher_test.go} - ingress/mux/{residency_gate.go,residency_gate_test.go} - docs/compliance/policy-contracts.md - installer/migrations/{migrate_policy_contracts.go} - charts/rbac-policy-bundle/values.yaml (feature flag) Review and feedback (selected): - Priya Rao (reviewer): "Can we avoid storing full key material in the ledger? auditors only need key id and region plus signed assertion." Author reply: "We only store key id + region + signed assertion; comments updated to clarify and added storage schema test." - Marta Alvarez: "We need explicit TTL and retention config per tenant for privacy/legal requests." Author reply: "Added tenant_retention_days in ledger config; added a migration that backfills TTL for existing tenants in the cohort." - Liam O'Connell: "How do we ensure operator-scoped roles can't escalate via policy contracts?" Author reply: "Policy contracts compile into RBACv2 constraints that are enforced in the PDP; operator roles are evaluated with a deny-first merge. Added unit tests covering operator override attempts." - Security review note (from sec-eng): "Signer key rotation must be audited; document added and rotation integrated into KMS provisioning flow." Author reply: "Rotation docs and a rotation e2e added; ledger exposes signer metadata in /v1/status." Billing/usage & telemetry: - Added metrics: policy_contracts.compiled, ledger.append.success, ledger.append.fail, residency_gate.denied, residency_gate.allowed. - Added trace spans for ledger-sign and commitment-attach with sampling tag policy_contract=true. Migration plan (operator-facing): - Phase 0: feature flag off. - Phase 1: enable read-only contract compilation and dry-run logging for 1% tenants. - Phase 2: enable enforcement for a small cohort; monitor metric residency_gate.denied and audit enrichment volume. - Phase 3: expand to 50% then 100% and deprecate legacy shim. Backout plan included. Observability: dashboards added in docs/dashboards/policy_contracts.json and alert policy: residency_gate.denied_rate > 0.1% sustained triggers P1. Security and compliance considerations: - Ledger entries are signed; access to ledger read API is restricted to compliance personas; retention configurable per-tenant; access logs forwarded to audit pipeline. - No plaintext key material stored. - Contract DSL reviewed for injection/resolution attack surfaces; compiler sandboxed. Docs and operator guide: - docs/compliance/policy-contracts.md with example workflows and remediation steps. - docs/upgrade/migration_steps.md with CLI helpers. Merge and rollout: - Merged via squash and merge after approvals. Merge commit: "Introduce policy contracts, commitment ledger, and residency gate (squash)". Notes and follow-ups: - Follow-up tasks added: ENG-8810 (policy-explain endpoint), SEC-345 (attestation of ledger signer), PM-402 enable customer-facing contract templates. - Suggested future work: compact zk-friendly proof for commitments; further KMS multi-provider cross-checks. Overall impact: This change centralizes compliance guardrails into an auditable, reviewable contract model that ties RBACv2 assertions to KMS choices and audit entries. It provides a clear, staged migration path and operator-facing controls required for region-pin and residency SLAs.

title: Coordinated credential handoff, compact retry presets, and generator stream ergonomics across SDKs
preview:
Motivation: We keep getting support tickets and cross-SDK bugs around: (1) credential handoff semantics when a streaming connection needs to rotate/refresh tokens mid-session, (2) divergent ergonomic surfaces for generator/async-iterator based streaming in each SDK, and (3) inconsistent retry presets that lead to surprising latency/cost tradeoffs in low-latency apps. This PR centralizes the contract in the monorepo and ships coordinated changes for the TypeScript/Python/Go SDKs and runtime float so customers can opt into a single predictable behaviour.

What this PR does (high-level): Add a CredentialHandoff contract (lightweight interface + reference implementation), introduce a new compact retry preset set (compact-1/2/3) tuned for short-sequence, latency-sensitive workloads, and unify the public generator/async-iterator surface across SDKs so streaming ergonomics are consistent and migration paths are obvious. This branch contains three smaller commits for the mono-repo: (A) contract + docs + tests, (B) SDK adapters and examples in ts/py/go packages, (C) version bumps and release notes.

Compatibility story: The default behaviour is backward compatible. SDKs expose opt-in flags: `credentialHandoff: 'auto' | 'explicit'` and `retryPreset: 'standard' | 'compact-2'`. Existing consumers continue to operate without change. The generator surface adds new helpers (e.g. `streamToAsyncGenerator`) that are additive wrappers; we also include a short migration note in the changelog.

Testing & verification: unit tests added for the handoff state machine, e2e integration test that simulates token expiry during a 90s long-stream request (CI runs a mocked auth server), and cross-SDK interop smoke tests that run TS -> runtime -> Go client scenarios. Local perf bench indicates compact-3 reduces median per-token latency by ~8% on short sequences due to reduced retry delays and more aggressive early-fails for known 4xx cases.

Follow-ups: coordinate with infra to roll kernel backoff tuning (ENG-2110), and schedule a docs rollout with the Developer Experience team to publish migration examples and a short cookbook (ENG-2199).

title: consolidate-credential-prefetch-and-stream-api-hygiene
preview: Motivation: multiple SDKs had duplicated fragile logic around credential prefetch/lease warmup and slightly divergent streaming ergonomics which caused intermittent auth races and inconsistent telemetry labels across Python/TS/Go. This PR centralizes prefetch/backoff presets, tightens stream lifecycle semantics, and harmonizes telemetry tags so multi-language integrations show coherent traces and cost attribution. Scope: 1) introduce a small cross-SDK contract (neutral JSON blob) that describes a credential prefetch profile (ttl_target_ms, warmup_window_ms, jitter_pct) and wire it into each SDK's credential manager; 2) surface a lightweight StreamHandle.close(reason) API in Python and TS and align Go CloseWithReason to match semantics (idempotent, synchronous drain then close); 3) add adaptive retry presets for auth failures (fast-fail under 401->refresh->retry) while preserving existing backoff defaults for network errors; 4) unify telemetry labels emitted by SDKs (session_id, cred_profile, retry_phase) and add an opt-in debug flag that enables per-request credential lifecycle spans; 5) bump SDK package minor versions (py 1.12.0, ts 2.4.1, go 1.5.0) and update changelogs. Risk and mitigation: exposing a common prefetch contract required verifying no consumer relied on previous implicit timing; we guarded rollout with environment-controlled feature-flag (REDWOOD_SDK_PREFETCH_V2) and added comprehensive integration tests. Backward compatibility: if the feature flag is off, SDKs behave exactly as before. Tests: added unit tests for credential window math, E2E smoke for stream close semantics in CI. Rollout: staged release — first neural infra and internal apps enabled the flag for two days, monitoring showed no auth regressions, then general availability. See release notes. Related work: follows ENG-9023 (credential resilience) and ENG-9102 (stream ergonomics parity).

title: Add Optimization Config change permissions + operator roles (apply/promote/rollback)
preview: Summary: Introduces explicit RBAC permissions for the Optimization Config lifecycle (create/validate/apply/promote/rollback) and adds two opinionated roles used by Console + control plane workflows. This is part of the Safe apply + rollback mechanism project to ensure rollback is both auditable and access-controlled. Motivation: Today, optimization config changes are implicitly covered by broad “platform operator” capabilities in some environments, and not consistently modeled in Private deployments. Enterprise customers and Security review require least-privilege roles with clear separation between “can propose” and “can execute rollback in prod.” Changes included: 1) New permissions: optimization_config.read, optimization_config.create_version, optimization_config.validate, optimization_config.diff, optimization_config.apply, optimization_config.promote, optimization_config.rollback, optimization_config.override_gates (break-glass), optimization_config.manage_rbac (internal only). 2) New roles: optimization_change_operator (intended for on-call + platform operators executing safe apply/rollback within an approved scope; includes read/diff/validate/apply/rollback; does NOT include promote-by-default to reduce accidental broad rollouts), optimization_change_admin (intended for senior operators; includes promote + override_gates; still does not include manage_rbac). 3) Updates to policy docs: clarified mapping from Console actions (“Rollback” button, “Promote to production”, “Override gates”) to permissions and recommended reviewer/approval process. 4) Tests: added rego/policy tests + golden snapshots covering allow/deny cases for Hosted/Dedicated/Private personas, including a regression test for INT-3390 (operator could not see rollback button due to missing permission). Backwards compatibility: No default widening of access; existing roles are unchanged except for documentation noting recommended migration. Control plane/Console will explicitly request these permissions when the feature flag is enabled. Rollout plan: Land policy bundle first; then enable in staging; then gate Console button visibility on optimization_config.rollback + server-side authz checks in control plane. Notes: “override_gates” is explicitly separate and intended for internal break-glass flows only (guarded by additional approval and audited separately).

title: traffic burst shielding and graceful-degradation rollout guard
preview:
Motivation: we see a class of incidents where sudden client-driven traffic bursts (spikes in request ingress slope) overwhelm KV-cache memory and batching heuristics, producing high tail latency and queueing even when nominal throughput is within capacity. Existing canary detectors focus on gradual regressions or static threshold crossings; they tend to either miss very short sharp bursts or trigger hard rollbacks that are disruptive for live rollouts.

This PR introduces 'Burst Shielding' — a new detector + graded mitigation rollout-guard that: 1) detects sudden traffic bursts using a fused signal of p99 latency delta, request ingress slope, request queue depth, and per-device KV cache eviction rate; 2) classifies burst severity with a Nyquist-inspired adaptive smoothing and causal-change detector to avoid false positives on regular traffic ramps; 3) applies graded, time-bound mitigations that prefer graceful degradation over hard rollback: scale temporary concurrency tokens, disable batching for affected routes, route a percentage of traffic to a low-latency variant (quantized/FP16), and raise an alert with suggested manual actions.

What this change does (high level):
- Adds a new detector implementation: perf_canary/detectors/traffic_burst_detector.py that computes a fused burst score and severity banding. Uses EWMA slope estimation + causal change point test to detect sub-minute bursts.
- Adds an orchestration policy: perf_canary/policies/burst_shield_policy.py that defines mitigation steps per severity and a cooldown window.
- Adds config and rollout guard integration: deployments/canary/burst_shield.yaml (tunable knobs: slope_window, score_thresholds, mitigation_sequence).
- Adds telemetry: metrics/telemetry.md and exporters for burst_score, kv_eviction_rate, ingress_slope, burst_mitigation_state.
- Adds integration tests and an end-to-end harness: tests/integration/test_burst_shield.py that simulates traffic bursts at varying ramp rates.
- Adds runbook and docs: docs/runbooks/burst-shield.md and a short guide in docs/canary-patterns.md.
- Small runtime changes: a light-weight KV eviction metric hook in serving/runtime/kv_stats.c and a debounced batching toggle in serving/runtime/batching_controller.go.

Checklist: 
- [x] Detector implementation and unit tests
- [x] Policy orchestration and integration tests
- [x] CI flakiness investigation (initial failing e2e)
- [x] Docs and runbook written
- [x] Linear items linked

Safety and rationale for graded mitigation: instead of immediately rejecting the rollout, Burst Shielding prefers ephemeral, reversible actions that reduce tail latency without discarding a rollout (e.g., disabling batching for the affected route reduces queuing at the cost of per-token CPU, which is acceptable for short-lived bursts). Routing to a low-latency variant is limited by percentage and time to reduce correctness risk.

Backward compatibility: default policy is off for existing canaries; enabling is opt-in via the manifest. No public API surface is changed.

References: ENG-7842 (detection spec), PM-221 (customer incident summary), internal perf notes (link omitted).

title: Add break-glass verification override guardrails + required audit annotations
preview: Title: Add break-glass verification override guardrails + required audit annotations. Context: As part of offline bundle signature verification, the installer now supports a policy-gated break-glass flag (see redwood-private-installer pr-24979). This PR adds cluster-side guardrails so that (1) break-glass cannot be enabled silently, (2) use is attributable and exportable via audit logs, and (3) only approved operators can authorize it. Summary of changes: (A) Adds a Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate + Constraint enforcing required annotations whenever break-glass is enabled via the Redwood Private install metadata ConfigMap (redwood-private/install-metadata) or Helm values rendered into that ConfigMap. Required annotations: redwood.ai/break-glass-ticket (SUP-/INT-/customer ticket id), redwood.ai/break-glass-reason (freeform, min 20 chars), redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-by (corporate email), redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-at (RFC3339 timestamp), redwood.ai/break-glass-expires-at (RFC3339; must be <= 72h from approved-at). Optional but recommended: redwood.ai/break-glass-approver-role (Security|ReleaseEng|SRE). (B) Adds a second constraint that denies enabling break-glass unless the requesting subject is in the allowlisted RBAC group (redwood:break-glass-approvers) or is a ServiceAccount with label redwood.ai/purpose=private-installer and bound to ClusterRole redwood-private-break-glass-approver. (C) Adds a policy exception mechanism for dev/test clusters only: if Namespace has label redwood.ai/environment=dev and redwood.ai/policy-exceptions contains break-glass-guardrails, constraints run in "warn" mode and include a warning message; default remains "deny" for all other environments. (D) Adds a small conftest test suite covering: missing annotations, malformed timestamps, expiry > 72h, approver not allowlisted, and the dev warn-only path. Why this approach: We want verification to fail closed by default but still support emergency installs when signatures cannot be verified due to environmental constraints (time skew, trust store mismatch, etc.). The installer already logs local audit markers; these policies ensure the cluster also has consistent, queryable attribution for any break-glass activation, and prevent accidental enablement by automation. Implementation notes: Policies target the install-metadata ConfigMap because it is the canonical place we already read for support bundles and audit export mapping. We intentionally avoid requiring outbound connectivity or external approvals (air-gapped), but we require the metadata fields that support and security will request during incident response and compliance evidence collection. Files / areas changed: policy/gatekeeper/templates/redwoodprivatebreakglass_annotations_template.yaml; policy/gatekeeper/constraints/redwoodprivatebreakglass_annotations_constraint.yaml; policy/gatekeeper/templates/redwoodprivatebreakglass_approver_template.yaml; policy/gatekeeper/constraints/redwoodprivatebreakglass_approver_constraint.yaml; rbac/clusterroles/redwood-private-break-glass-approver.yaml; rbac/clusterrolebindings/redwood-private-break-glass-approver-binding.yaml; tests/conftest/break_glass_annotations_test.rego; tests/fixtures/install-metadata-configmap.yaml; README.md (break-glass section). Illustrative snippet (ConstraintTemplate logic, shortened): "violation[{"msg": msg}] { input.review.kind.kind == "ConfigMap"; input.review.object.metadata.name == "install-metadata"; input.review.object.metadata.namespace == "redwood-private"; input.review.object.data["break_glass_enabled"] == "true"; missing := {"redwood.ai/break-glass-ticket", "redwood.ai/break-glass-reason", "redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-by", "redwood.ai/break-glass-approved-at", "redwood.ai/break-glass-expires-at"} - {k | input.review.object.metadata.annotations[k]}; count(missing) > 0; msg := sprintf("break-glass enabled but missing required annotations: %v", [missing]) }". Testing: make policy-lint; make test-conftest; validated against a local kind cluster with Gatekeeper v3.14. CI: gatekeeper-schema-check, conftest, yamllint all green. Rollout: This is a no-op unless Gatekeeper is installed (standard for regulated Private installs). For existing clusters, apply templates/constraints first (will only affect future changes to install metadata). Related: ENG-4824 (break-glass design), ENG-4829 (audit event schema), SUP-31977 (support guidance).

title: Introduce per-prompt temporal baseline probe, drift-aware CI gate, and lightweight counterfactual replayer
preview:
Motivation: Some prompt-set regressions we observe are intermittent and tied to recent temporal shifts in training data or model weights. Existing cohort/anchor approaches flag regressions but often require manual baseline selection or suffer from noise when the underlying distribution evolves. This PR adds a Per-Prompt Temporal Baseline (PTB) probe that maintains short- and long-window baselines per prompt, a drift-aware CI gate that reasons about recent baseline movement before failing a job, and a lightweight counterfactual replayer used to prioritize regressions for human review.

Design summary: The PTB probe (eval_harness.probes.ptb_probe) computes exponentially weighted aggregates (EWMA) of configured metrics per prompt across two windows: short (default 1d) and long (default 30d). The probe exposes three signals: absolute delta (short vs long), variance ratio, and a stability score combining token-level variance and aggregated metric delta. The CI gate (ci/hooks/ptb_gate.py) uses these signals to decide fail/soft-fail behavior: if absolute delta exceeds the hard threshold we fail; if delta is between soft and hard thresholds we emit a soft-fail with attached diagnoses and optionally enqueue counterfactual replays.

Counterfactual replayer: A new lightweight replayer (eval_harness.tools.counterfactual_replayer) can replay a small, latency-bounded subset of sampled historical inputs against the PR head model variant and against a pinned stable model to produce a prioritized list of likely regressions. The replayer is intentionally cheap (bounded tokens and sample size) so it can run in CI without blowing budgets. It includes deterministic seeding options for reproducibility.

APIs and config: Introduces a new probe registration entry in probes/registry.yaml with tunable params (short_window, long_window, ewma_alpha, soft_threshold, hard_threshold, replay_budget). CI integration exposes these flags via .github/workflows/eval.yml as optional job inputs. Default gate behavior is non-blocking (soft-fail) for self-serve runs; for release branches the gate is enabled as hard-fail.

Testing and verification: Added unit tests covering EWMA convergence, signal thresholds, and replayer determinism (tests/test_ptb_probe.py). Added an integration smoke job in ci/integration/ptb_smoke.yml that runs the probe on a synthetic prompt set and validates gating behavior. Local benchmarks show the replayer adds ~8% CI runtime under the default replay_budget=50 samples.

Rollout plan: Start with opt-in on main via a feature flag (env PTB_GATE_MODE=soft). After two weeks of telemetry (false-positive rate, queue depth, latency) we plan to flip the default to hard for release branches only and surface auto-created prioritized tickets in Linear for prompts with persistent hard-fail signals.

Migration notes: No breaking changes to existing probe interfaces. Existing registries will pick up the PTB probe by default but the gate remains opt-in. See docs/eval/ptb.md for configuration and troubleshooting.

Related: Linear ENG-1984 (per-prompt stability monitoring), PM-422 (release gate automation). Support ticket SUP-6123 provided customer repro prompting this work.

title: Add trigger endpoint for Optimize quality gating (config diff + baseline)
preview: Context: Optimize needs a first-class way to run offline evals against a proposed serving config change (batching/caching/quantization profile) before allowing apply/rollout. This PR adds a new API endpoint and job plumbing in eval-harness to accept a config diff + baseline reference, execute the requested gate tier against selected prompt sets, and return structured metrics plus artifact pointers suitable for Console/Optimize consumption. Endpoint: POST /api/v1/gates/trigger (auth: service-to-service token; scoped to optimize-gating). Request (JSON): gate_tier ("smoke"|"full"), model_id, baseline (baseline_model_version + baseline_runtime_config_ref), proposed (runtime_config_diff, optional proposed_model_version), prompt_sets (ids), sample_count (optional; defaults per tier), run_metadata (route_id, account_id, region, commit_sha, idempotency_key). Validation: limits request payload size, rejects unknown diff keys, enforces that baseline ref is immutable (no "latest"), and checks prompt set ids exist and are enabled for gating. Execution: enqueues a GateRun job to the existing eval runner queue, pins model + runtime settings for the duration of the run, and writes an immutable run record with normalized inputs for audit/debug. Response: 202 Accepted with gate_run_id + status_url + estimated_duration_s; once complete, GET /api/v1/gates/runs/{id} returns status, summary metrics (per prompt set and overall), and artifact pointers (artifact_store_uri + optional signed_url when allowed), including: raw per-example outputs, rubric/score breakdowns, and a compact "comparison.json" (baseline vs proposed deltas). Artifacts: stored under artifact_store_uri prefix gates/{gate_run_id}/..., with retention aligned to existing eval artifact policy; Optimize can deep-link using gate_run_id. Observability: emits gate_trigger_requests_total and gate_run_enqueue_latency_ms (tags: gate_tier, model_id, region, caller=optimize) and attaches trace/span attributes for correlation from Optimize -> eval-harness. Tests: adds contract tests for request validation + schema stability, a fake runner integration test that asserts correct pinning of baseline/proposed configs, and golden JSON snapshots for the run detail response. Notes: This PR does not implement flake scoring/rerun aggregation (tracked in pr-418); response schema includes an optional flake_score field but it is not populated yet. Rollout: endpoint is behind feature flag OPTIMIZE_GATING_TRIGGER_API and requires allowlisted client identity; we can safely deploy without enabling any callers.

title: Require incident_type/subtype on create; auto-resolve owner + escalation channel
preview:
Context: As part of the Reliability Program “Incident taxonomy + ownership mapping”, we’re making incident_type a required field at incident creation time, and we want the bot to resolve an owner team consistently (single primary owner) to reduce time-to-engage and improve reporting quality.

This PR updates the Slack modal + slash command flow to require incident_type and incident_subtype (from the v1 allowlist), then performs owner resolution (type/subtype -> primary owner + escalation channel) and posts the result into the incident channel. The goal is: (1) classification is required and auditable, (2) owner is consistently derived from a versioned mapping, and (3) the IC gets an immediate “who owns this” + “where to escalate” suggestion.

User-facing changes:
- /incident create now blocks submission until incident_type is selected.
- incident_subtype is required when the type has subtypes; otherwise the field is hidden.
- After incident is created, bot posts a short summary: incident_type/subtype, resolved owner team, escalation channel, and a reminder that IC can override classification if needed.
- If mapping is missing (e.g., new subtype), we fall back to SRE on-call owner and label classification as “needs-mapping”; bot also files a lightweight internal warning event.

Implementation notes:
- Added TaxonomyAllowlist (static JSON in-repo for now) with strict validation + display labels.
- Added OwnerResolver that consults the mapping (fetched via config service; local fallback for dev/test).
- Added “override owner” admin-only action in the incident channel (logs audit event; does not change the canonical mapping).
- Added structured fields to incident record: incident_type, incident_subtype, owner_team, escalation_channel, owner_resolution_source.

Out of scope:
- Auto-suggest incident_type from symptoms (tracked separately).
- Retrofitting historical incidents (backfill job lives in redwood/observability).

Rollout:
- Behind feature flag: incident_taxonomy_required_fields.
- Enabled for eng-sre workspace first; then global after 1 week if no issues.

Checklist:
- [x] Slack modal validation for required fields
- [x] Owner resolution + fallback behavior
- [x] Audit/event logging for overrides
- [x] Unit tests for allowlist parsing + resolver
- [x] E2E test for create flow
- [x] Docs snippet in /help output

Screenshots/GIF: attached in PR (Slack modal with incident_type dropdown + subtype conditional).
