title: Policy engine: add priority classes and per-route budgets (deterministic decision logging + validation)
preview: Summary: This PR extends the policy engine to support (a) explicit priority classes (enterprise/standard/best-effort/internal) and (b) per-route budgets that can be consumed deterministically during admission control / overload protection. It also adds deterministic decision logging (stable reason codes + structured fields) and a schema validation layer to keep policies auditable for enterprise escalations. Motivation: Hot-route capacity protection requires a single, explainable place to answer: "given tenant, route group, and current capacity signals, should we admit / throttle / shed / fallback". We need to do this with minimal per-request overhead and without introducing high-cardinality telemetry. This PR focuses on correctness + determinism; performance tuning (memoization/batched lookups) will follow. What’s in scope: 1) Policy schema additions: - priority_class: enum {INTERNAL, ENTERPRISE, STANDARD, BEST_EFFORT} (default STANDARD for backward compatibility) - route_group: canonical name for grouping endpoints (e.g., chat_completions, embeddings, rerank) - budgets: optional per route_group budgets keyed by priority class (requests/sec + burst + optional concurrency cap) - actions: allow | throttle | shed | fallback (fallback gated; final enforcement happens in gateway/routing) 2) Policy evaluation contract: - input includes: tenant_id, tier, route, route_group, region, and coarse capacity signals (queue_depth_bucket, gpu_saturation_bucket, scheduler_lag_bucket) - output includes: decision, decision_reason, retry_after_ms (optional), chosen_priority_class, matched_rule_id, and a stable decision_hash for auditability 3) Deterministic decision logging: - new "policy_decision" log event emitted by the policy engine with stable, low-cardinality fields - reason codes normalized to align with 429 semantics work in gateway (QUOTA_EXCEEDED vs OVERLOAD_SHED vs ADMISSION_THROTTLE vs FALLBACK_APPLIED) 4) Validation + guardrails: - schema validation on policy load (reject unknown enums/invalid windows) - explicit checks preventing per-tenant/per-route unconstrained overrides unless allowlisted (prevents accidental fleet-wide noisy-neighbor exceptions) - budget sanity checks (burst >= rate, non-negative, max caps) 5) Tests: - golden tests for deterministic output (given the same input policy + request context, decision_hash and reason fields must match) - validation tests covering invalid policies and non-backwards-compatible changes - basic evaluation tests covering priority ordering and budget exhaustion behavior Notes on backwards compatibility: - Existing policies without priority_class/route_group continue to evaluate as STANDARD with route_group derived from route mapping if available; if not available, route_group="unknown" and budgets are not applied. - Decision logging is added but does not change existing endpoints; log event is separate from request logs. Implementation details: - Added a policy schema version bump (v7 -> v8) with a translator that fills defaults on load. - Added a small canonical route->route_group mapping table co-located with schema; enforcement services should treat route_group as authoritative once provided in request context. - Budget accounting is modeled as an input signal ("budget_remaining_bucket") for now; actual counters are managed by gateway/routing. This PR sets the language/types and ensures evaluation is deterministic. Security/Access: - Deterministic decision logging includes tenant_id but is restricted to internal logs; no customer PII. - No new external endpoints. Follow-ups (tracked separately): - Performance: caching / batch lookups for high-QPS (ENG-4924/ENG-4925 dependent, plus upcoming perf PR). - Unify reason-code taxonomy and headers with gateway implementation once landed (paired with Amara/Cole). - Add admin-only debug endpoint to inspect decisions (INT-3302) once access controls are in place. References: - Linear: ENG-4923 - Docs/Design: Hot-route capacity protection design + ADR (see Confluence links in ENG-4923) - Related PRs: gateway 429 reason codes + routing admission control will consume decision_reason + priority_class.

title: Slab concatenation + FlashMixer kernel with async KV coalescer and continuous-batching horizon
preview: Context: Operator-level profiling in the last quarter showed sustained stalls on small-to-medium sequence lengths due to scatter/gather overheads and suboptimal KV warmup for bursty traffic. This PR introduces a new attention strategy (SlabConcatenate + FlashMixer microkernel) that reduces per-token overhead for heterogeneous sequence distributions and a lightweight async-KV coalescer that amortizes KV cache population across micro-batches. Motivation: 1) Reduce small-batch latency and increase throughput at mid-sequence lengths (256-2048) where customer workloads spend most of their tokens. 2) Retain deterministic rotary/rope semantics while enabling a fused concatenate path that avoids extra memcpy for tiled KV layouts. 3) Improve continuous batching horizon so runtime can opportunistically stretch batching without violating latency SLOs. What this change does: - Introduces FlashMixer microkernel: a variant of Flash-attention style tiling that mixes slab-concatenated Q/K/V regions to minimize memory fragmentation and enable a single fused rotary application pass. Optimized for 8-16 head widths on A100/RTX7x. - Adds SlabConcatenate path to the kernel selector with heuristics based on sequence length distribution and current batch skew metric. This path fuses concatenation, rotary application, and QK matmul into one kernel for contiguous slabs. - Implements Async KV Coalescer: a runtime stage that coalesces concurrent KV writes from overlapping micro-batches into a single GPU write and exposes a KV warmup metric. Coalescer has backpressure safety to avoid overpopulation for very long sequences. - Extends continuous batching horizon: runtime now computes an elastic horizon that trades a small p99 latency tail against higher throughput; this is governed by a new config: continuous_batching.max_horizon_ms and continuous_batching.tail_target_pct. - Preservation of model semantics: fused rotary/rope ops are designed to be numerically identical to the existing path (FP16 accumulation path validated). Testing & benchmarks: - Microbenchmarks (internal harness) show 9-14% throughput uplift and 6-10% median latency reduction at seq=512/1024 for T5-like models and ~12% on LLaMA-variant runs with head dim 64. - KV-heavy chat workload (continuous small messages) showed 18% cost improvement due to reduced redundant KV writes. - Extensive regression tests validate logits and sampling for baseline prompts and prompt sets in eval harness. Rollout plan: gated feature behind runtime flag slab_concat.enabled; rollout to canary clusters for 2 weeks; if no regressions, flip on in dedicated pools with auto-fallback to legacy path. Related: ENG-4821 (kv warmup telemetry), ENG-4899 (batching SLOs), SUP-3210 (customer-reported chat latency spike). Notes: this PR intentionally avoids global allocator changes and keeps the fused kernel opt-in. The body below includes a short appendix with file-level highlights and a summary of reviewer discussion and CI details.

title: Introduce proportional failure heatmap and context coverage CI hook
preview: Motivation: some regressions surface only in high-volume contexts or in narrow prompt cohorts; existing eval gates treat each failing example equally which underweights production impact. This PR introduces a proportional failure heatmap and a context-coverage metric that (1) weights failures by frequency and severity, (2) surfaces top-left hotspots in a time-decayed heatmap, and (3) produces a single coverage score used by the CI hook. Design: we cluster prompts into contextual cohorts using the existing prompt fingerprinting + semantic shingle embedding. For each cohort we compute a weighted failure score = sum_{examples in cohort}(failure_weight * recency_decay * frequency_factor). Frequency comes from the sampled traffic histogram (or sample proxy during CI). Severity is derived from configured eval metrics (exact-match, BLEU drop, hallucination flag), mapped to a 1-5 weight. We render a heatmap matrix: rows = cohorts, cols = failure types/buckets, cell = normalized proportional score. The context-coverage score is a normalized aggregate that penalizes under-tested or high-impact cohorts. CI behavior: runs as a post-eval hook. Default is soft-fail (annotate PR and open an advisory alert) and will fail the CI only when coverage < 0.6 AND proportional failure mass > threshold for two consecutive runs. There is an opt-in strict mode in repo eval.yaml to convert soft-fails into hard blocks. Implementation summary: - New hook entry point: eval_harness/hooks/proportional_failure_heatmap.py - Cohort sampler & cache adaptor: eval_harness/samplers/contextual_cohort_sampler.py - Heatmap renderer + export: eval_harness/reporting/coverage_heatmap_v1.py - CLI/backfill helper: tools/heatmap_backfill.py - Dashboard defs: infra/dashboards/coverage-heatmap.json - Unit + integration tests: tests/hooks/test_proportional_heatmap.py + tests/samplers/test_contextual_cohort.py Backwards compatibility: hook uses existing eval outputs; no changes required to model outputs or existing prompt sets. Rollout plan: 1) Merge in soft-fail mode, enable on canary branch for 48 hours (automated job), 2) run backfill for last 30 days using tools/heatmap_backfill.py to populate dashboards, 3) after observing false positive rate < 4% and stable signal for 1 week, flip opt-in projects to strict gating. Checklist: - [x] Unit tests - [x] Integration test (CI job) - [x] Dashboard JSON added - [x] Documentation snippet added to docs/hooks.md - [x] Linear tickets linked (ENG-2345) - [x] Backfill tool included

title: Implement prompt fingerprint ledger and replay-safe CI enforcer
preview:
Motivation: Flaky eval failures at the prompt-set level continue to surface from subtle dataset composition drift (sampling, paraphrase swaps, and tokenization edge cases) rather than model regressions. This PR implements a compact on-disk ledger and a CI-side enforcer that (1) records stable fingerprints for prompt-sets+seed+model-config, (2) compares new runs against the ledger to flag composition-driven variance, and (3) provides a replay-safe enforcement mode that permits human review and controlled backfills before a hard CI failure.

Design summary: The fingerprint is a deterministic, salted hash that combines prompt identifiers, normalized prompt text, canonical tokenization signature, run-seed, and a lightweight output sketch (sampled token-level checksums plus aggregated metrics). A new ledger store (local by default; support for S3/GCS) holds one canonical entry per evaluated prompt-set+baseline ID. The CI enforcer (eval_ci_enforcer.py) is a thin adapter that runs after eval jobs complete: it computes fingerprints for the run, diffs against the ledger, and decides pass/warn/fail according to policy. Policies are configurable via the existing eval configuration (eval_config.yaml) with three modes: off, warn-only, and replay-safe-enforce. Replay-safe-enforce: when mismatches are detected, the job is marked 'blocked' and an automated backfill job is queued (if configured) or a human review workflow is created in Linear.

What changed (high level): added ledger implementation and on-disk format, CLI helpers to snapshot and backfill prompt-sets, CI enforcer adapter, configuration knobs, metrics and tracing spans, unit and integration tests, and PR-level documentation. No changes to existing eval metric implementations. The ledger schema is intentionally conservative to remain forward-compatible.

Checklist:
- [x] Fingerprint algorithm implementation (stable hashing + normalization)
- [x] Ledger write/read with S3/GCS/local adapters
- [x] CI adapter that integrates into our GitHub Actions eval workflow
- [x] Opt-in replay-safe-enforce policy and Linear backfill creation
- [x] Unit tests for fingerprinting and ledger operations
- [x] Integration test with a small synthetic prompt-set to validate end-to-end blocking/backfill path
- [x] Documentation section and examples in docs/eval/ledger.md

Compatibility & rollout: Default is off. We will enable warn-only for the old smoke-eval workflow for two weeks to collect telemetry, then enable replay-safe-enforce for new eval runs on the staging pipeline. The ledger migration is read-on-write; existing evals are not retroactively fingerprinted unless a backfill is triggered.

Performance: Fingerprinting adds ~15-40ms per prompt at 1k prompts (I/O dominated) in local mode and ~60-150ms when writing to remote object storage; these are small compared to token-generation time in typical LLM evals. The ledger size is compact (fingerprint + metadata ≈ 200B per prompt-set entry).

Operational notes: metrics emitted under eval.fingerprint.* (counts, mismatch_rate, backfill_queued) and tracing spans added to eval-run traces. The CI adapter annotates PRs with a succinct mismatch summary and a link to the ledger diff viewer.

Rollout plan: staged enablement (warn-only -> replay-safe-enforce opt-in -> org-wide enforcement) and a one-week monitoring window after each step. If mismatch_rate spikes from baseline, automatically open a Linear ticket assigned to the owning team for triage.

title: Add KMS throttle budget + request backpressure to prevent KMS saturation under spikes
preview:
Context: We’ve seen intermittent elevated 5xx correlated with upstream KMS throttling (SUP-19377) and a near-miss retry amplification event that required a hotfix (pr-18563). While the wrapper now has sane retry/jitter defaults and circuit breaking, we still had a failure mode where a large burst of decrypt/envelope ops (e.g. cache cold start + traffic spike) could saturate KMS and extend the incident blast radius.

This PR introduces an explicit "KMS throttle budget" and integrates KMS calls with the existing request admission/backpressure path so we shed/queue work earlier rather than stampeding KMS.

What’s included (high level): (1) Per-process token-bucket budget for KMS operations: A lightweight budget that is decremented on KMS requests and refilled at a configured rate. When depleted, KMS calls are delayed (bounded) or rejected with a typed error depending on caller policy. This keeps concurrency and request rate within a predictable envelope even if upstream is degraded. (2) Adaptive refill + penalty on throttling: When KMS returns throttling (e.g., AWS ThrottlingException / 429 equivalents), we apply an immediate penalty to the budget refill rate for a short window. This quickly reduces pressure and avoids repeated throttles. (3) Backpressure integration: The KMS client wrapper now plugs into `admission.Controller` so callers can either: (a) block up to `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` and then fail fast, or (b) fail immediately (for latency-sensitive paths) with a clear error category (`KmsBudgetExhausted`). (4) Metrics + tracing: Exposes budget state and decisions, and tags spans with the backpressure outcome.

Config knobs (all have safe defaults; prod guardrails still enforced by pr-18502): - `kms.budget.refill_rps` (default: 200 ops/sec) - `kms.budget.burst` (default: 400 ops) - `kms.backpressure.max_wait_ms` (default: 50ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_window_ms` (default: 30000ms) - `kms.budget.throttle_penalty_factor` (default: 0.5)

Behavioral notes: - This budget is per-process (not global) by design. It limits local amplification and works well with our horizontal scaling/admission control. If we need global limits later, we can layer it via control plane config + per-pool budgets. - We do NOT silently drop decrypts. If we can’t meet the wait bound, we return a typed error and let the caller’s policy decide whether to fail the request, fall back, or rely on cached material (where allowed). - When combined with the circuit breaker, the system now transitions more cleanly: budget starts slowing requests first; if KMS remains degraded, the breaker opens and callers follow degrade-mode policy.

Implementation details: - `kmsbudget.Bucket`: token-bucket with penalty hooks; uses monotonic clock; jittered sleeps to avoid herd effects at refill boundaries. - `kmsclient.Client`: wraps `Encrypt/Decrypt/GenerateDataKey` calls with `Acquire(ctx)`; all outcomes emit structured logs and metrics. - `admission.Controller`: added a dependency-specific limiter hook (KMS only for now) so we can reuse the backpressure path without leaking KMS specifics into product code.

Metrics added: - `redwood_kms_budget_tokens{env,region,service}` (gauge) - `redwood_kms_budget_acquire_total{outcome="immediate|waited|timeout|rejected"}` (counter) - `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms` (histogram) - `redwood_kms_throttle_penalty_active` (gauge) - Existing `redwood_kms_errors_total{category=...}` is now incremented for `BudgetExhausted` and `BackpressureTimeout` (maps to the canonical taxonomy).

Example log fields (new/standardized): `kms_op`, `kms_budget_outcome`, `kms_budget_wait_ms`, `kms_budget_tokens_after`, `kms_error_category`, `trace_id`.

Testing: - Unit tests for token bucket correctness and penalty behavior (including time skews). - Integration harness updated to simulate 429/throttling and verify that request rate to the KMS stub decreases within 2s and that tail latency stays bounded (ties into ENG-4825). - Added a regression test ensuring we don’t exceed retry+budget wait combined latency cap.

Rollout plan: - Behind `kms.backpressure.enabled` (default ON in staging, OFF in prod until we flip per environment). - Canary in us-east Dedicated + Hosted data-plane: monitor `redwood_kms_budget_wait_ms p95`, KMS 429 rate, and request 5xx. - Flip prod flag gradually per region; rollback is a single flag change.

Checklist: - [x] SRE reviewed paging/threshold implications (no new pages by default; dashboards use derived panels) - [x] Security reviewed failure semantics (no weakening of envelope decrypt correctness) - [x] Added metrics + trace tags per observability conventions - [x] Added tests for throttle + backpressure scenarios

Follow-ups: - Consider exposing recommended budget defaults in the Private operator guide once we validate prod behavior across 2+ regions. - Evaluate whether control plane should push pool-specific budgets for Dedicated customers with known KMS quotas.

Related: ENG-4821, ENG-4825, ENG-4828; incident context SUP-19377; complements pr-18563 (retry storm hotfix), pr-18449 (circuit breaker), pr-18502 (config guardrails).

title: adaptive qk-kv fusion with prefetching and rotary interleave for long-context throughput
preview: Motivation: Long-context workloads (>=16k tokens) dominate several customer workloads. Existing FlashAttention and separate QK/KV kernels yield suboptimal memory traffic when serving long sequences with continuous batching and active KV caching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel strategy that fuses QK compute and KV accumulation paths where beneficial, plus a lightweight KV prefetcher and a rotary-interleave layout to reduce memory bandwidth and kernel launch overhead. Design: 1) New fused kernels (CUDA) that compute QK scores and simultaneously write/aggregate KV fragments for future decode steps. These kernels are selected when sequence-length, batch composition, and kv cache hit heuristics indicate net win. 2) Adaptive kernel dispatcher: runtime heuristics (histogram of seq lengths, batch fill factor, GPU occupancy) choose between FlashAttention variants, fused QK-KV path, or the legacy split kernels. 3) KV prefetcher: background thread schedules small prefetches of cache pages for the most likely next-tokens based on route-level bandit policy, reducing tail latency on cache-miss heavy workloads. 4) Rotary interleave: reorganizes rotary embeddings in-memory to make fused kernels use contiguous loads for q/k/v, reducing strided accesses on long context. Implementation notes: - CUDA kernels implemented in runtime/attention with careful register/block tuning for Ampere/Blackwell GPUs. - Dispatcher lives in runtime/attention/kernel_dispatcher.cc and exposes a policy hook for testing via env vars. - Continuous batcher updated to expose batch shape histograms and to annotate requests with expected next-token probabilities (used by prefetcher). - Small pinned-memory prefetch buffer added to runtime/kv_cache, with heuristics to avoid polluting hot-cache entries. Tests & validation: microbenchmarks (tests/benchmarks/attn_long_context.py) show median throughput gain of 18% on 32k-context benchmarks vs previous flash-attn-only baseline, p50 decode latency reduced 12% for batched streaming, and token-cost reduction of ~9% under production-like mixes due to fewer kernel launches and lower GPU memory traffic. We ran full GPU integration tests, perf canary, and private deployment smoke tests. Rollout plan: feature gated behind env REDWOOD_FEATURE_ADAPTIVE_FUSE and opt-in in Dedicated as default off for first 2 weeks. Safety: we added deterministic fallbacks and correctness unit tests comparing fused outputs to reference split-kernel outputs for a variety of dtype/seq-length/model-size combinations. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. See release_notes for user-visible improvements.
text: Motivation: Long-context workloads (>=16k tokens) dominate several customer workloads. Existing FlashAttention and separate QK/KV kernels yield suboptimal memory traffic when serving long sequences with continuous batching and active KV caching. This PR introduces an adaptive kernel strategy that fuses QK compute and KV accumulation paths where beneficial, plus a lightweight KV prefetcher and a rotary-interleave layout to reduce memory bandwidth and kernel launch overhead. Design: 1) New fused kernels (CUDA) that compute QK scores and simultaneously write/aggregate KV fragments for future decode steps. These kernels are selected when sequence-length, batch composition, and kv cache hit heuristics indicate net win. 2) Adaptive kernel dispatcher: runtime heuristics (histogram of seq lengths, batch fill factor, GPU occupancy) choose between FlashAttention variants, fused QK-KV path, or the legacy split kernels. 3) KV prefetcher: background thread schedules small prefetches of cache pages for the most likely next-tokens based on route-level bandit policy, reducing tail latency on cache-miss heavy workloads. 4) Rotary interleave: reorganizes rotary embeddings in-memory to make fused kernels use contiguous loads for q/k/v, reducing strided accesses on long context. Implementation notes: - CUDA kernels implemented in runtime/attention with careful register/block tuning for Ampere/Blackwell GPUs. - Dispatcher lives in runtime/attention/kernel_dispatcher.cc and exposes a policy hook for testing via env vars. - Continuous batcher updated to expose batch shape histograms and to annotate requests with expected next-token probabilities (used by prefetcher). - Small pinned-memory prefetch buffer added to runtime/kv_cache, with heuristics to avoid polluting hot-cache entries. Tests & validation: microbenchmarks (tests/benchmarks/attn_long_context.py) show median throughput gain of 18% on 32k-context benchmarks vs previous flash-attn-only baseline, p50 decode latency reduced 12% for batched streaming, and token-cost reduction of ~9% under production-like mixes due to fewer kernel launches and lower GPU memory traffic. We ran full GPU integration tests, perf canary, and private deployment smoke tests. Rollout plan: feature gated behind env REDWOOD_FEATURE_ADAPTIVE_FUSE and opt-in in Dedicated as default off for first 2 weeks. Safety: we added deterministic fallbacks and correctness unit tests comparing fused outputs to reference split-kernel outputs for a variety of dtype/seq-length/model-size combinations. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. See release_notes for user-visible improvements.

title: Throughput Sentinel: elasticity anchoring and microburst insulation for Dedicated fleets
preview:
Motivation: Dedicated fleet autoscalers at scale have repeatedly oscillated on short workload shifts and microbursts. Scale-up reactions are generally fine, but scale-down decisions occasionally evict capacity too aggressively and fail to reserve microburst headroom for tenants with bursty traffic, causing transient SLA slippage. This PR introduces a small, conservative control-plane component — the Throughput Sentinel — which provides an elasticity anchoring layer that biases autoscaler decisions using a ratcheted utilization view and a microburst insulation budget per tenant.

Design summary: The Throughput Sentinel is an opt-in controller that sits between the utilization sampler and the scale decision engine. Key concepts:
- Ratcheted utilization: maintain a short-term percentile (P90/P95 by config) utilization envelope per tenant/pool and never drop below the envelope for scale-down decisions until a configurable decay window passes.
- Elasticity anchoring: compute an anchoring factor (0..1) that dampens scale-down velocity based on recent utilization delta, tail latency, and warm-pool occupancy.
- Microburst insulation tokens: lightweight credit tokens allocated per tenant that represent reserved headroom for microbursts; tokens are consumed on accepted bursts and replenished over longer windows. Tokens prevent aggressive preemption of spare slots needed for short-lived spikes.
- Telemetry: expose new metrics (throughput_sentinel.ratchet_util, throughput_sentinel.anchor_factor, throughput_sentinel.tokens_remaining) and an aggregated microburst_accounting histogram to help diagnose burst-related throttles.

Checklist:
- [x] Controller implementation + unit tests
- [x] Autoscaler integration (hooks for anchor factor)
- [x] Telemetry + Prometheus rules and Grafana dashboard draft
- [x] E2E scenario tests (scale-down oscillation + synthetic microburst)
- [x] Feature flag gated (default OFF for existing customers)

Commits (high level):
- 3c7fa1d: introduce throughput sentinel controller, core state, and ratchet algorithm
- a9bf22e: autoscaler integration: apply anchor factor and token checks during proposed scale-down
- 5e1d8b4: telemetry, dashboards, tests, and configuration knobs

Files touched (high level):
- pkg/controller/throughput_sentinel/controller.go
- pkg/controller/throughput_sentinel/ratchet.go
- pkg/autoscaler/scale_decider/anchor_integration.go
- internal/telemetry/metrics_registry.go (added metrics & labels)
- infra/grafana/dashboards/throughput_sentinel.jsonnet
- test/e2e/fixtures/microburst_scenario.yaml
- helm/charts/redwood-tenant/values.yaml (feature flag)
- docs/ops/throughput-sentinel.md
- ci/verify/e2e-throughput-sentinel.sh

Behaviour and safety notes:
- Feature flag & rollout: the sentinel is disabled by default. A runtime flag can enable it per-cluster; rollout plan recommended: stage in non-prod -> subset of Dedicated pools -> all Dedicated pools.
- Fail-open: if sentinel cannot retrieve sufficient telemetry (e.g. aggregator outage) it will skip anchoring and let autoscaler behave as before to avoid global risk.
- Tunables: ratchet percentile, decay window, anchor_strength, token_replenish_window and per-tenant max_tokens are configurable via the existing autoscaler config map.

CI summary:

- Lint: passed (go vet/golangci-lint)
- Integration tests: initial run exposed a flake in e2e/microburst harness (timing), fixed by adjusting a wait condition in the test. Rerun: passed.
- Performance bench: lightweight local bench shows <3% extra CPU overhead on control-plane leader and negligible latency to scale decisions (~4ms median additional).

Review conversation (summary):
- Ethan Park: asked for explicit fallbacks when telemetry is missing and suggested a per-pool kill-switch. Also requested benchmark data for leader election/CPU. I added a fail-open path and a per-pool enable flag; benchmark attached.
- Priya Nair: requested clearer invariants to ensure the anchor doesn't prevent necessary scale-down after sustained idle periods. Added decay window and a conservative default anchor_strength=0.25; documented invariants in docs/ops/throughput-sentinel.md.
- Luca Bianchi: raised concern about cross-tenant fairness when tokens are scarce; proposed a weighted token allocation based on tenant commitment tier. Implemented a simple weight multiplier and left hooks for future policy modules.

Post-review changes:
- Added max_tokens enforcement and weight multiplier (defaults to 1.0)
- Added per-pool enablement flag in helm values
- Expanded telemetry tags to include pool_id and tenant_tier
- Tightened unit tests to exercise the fail-open path and token exhaustion edge cases

title: add-load-diffusion-prober-and-smooth-fallback-oracle
preview: Motivation: production rollouts occasionally experienced sharp, short-duration latency spikes when traffic shifts concentrated on a small set of worker shards. This PR introduces a traffic-aware Load Diffusion Prober and a Smooth Fallback Oracle to reduce false-positive rollbacks and provide graceful stall protection during bursty traffic. The Load Diffusion Prober continuously samples routing keys and estimates effective shard utilization and collision probability; when hotspots are detected it adjusts probe weights and issues soft-penalties instead of hard rollbacks. The Smooth Fallback Oracle consumes prober signals plus percentile-residual smoothing to decide fallback windows and maximum rollback budgets. Implementation: - lightweight prober module with opt-in sampling policy tied to rollout gates - reservoir-based diffusion estimator that adapts window size by observed burstiness - percentile residual smoother (EMA over 99th/95th) to avoid reacting to single-bucket spikes - integration layer with existing canary-evaluator and rollout-controller - feature flag and config knobs for dedicated vs shared pools Tests & validation: unit and integration tests for estimator math, simulations with synthetic burst traces, and a 48-hour production shadow run on staging showing 60% fewer unnecessary rollbacks and 30% lower 99th latency amplification during traffic bursts. Backwards compatibility: defaults are off; existing rollout logic remains unchanged unless feature flag enabled. Related: ENG-2381 (reduce noisy rollbacks), ENG-2399 (improve burst resilience). Checklist: [x] design doc linked [x] unit tests [x] integration tests [x] staging shadow run [x] dashboard and alert updates [x] docs update (console UI config).

title: ci: generate release graph and manifest validator for private-installer artifacts
preview: Motivation: CI for private installer releases lacked a machine-readable release graph and a manifest-level validator that ties signed artifacts (images, helm charts, tf modules) back to the release manifest. This PR adds a release-graph generator and a manifest-validator stage to the private-installer CI pipeline to ensure provenance, enable safer upgrades/rollbacks, and block unsigned/unaligned manifests from publishing. Summary of changes: - new pipeline step 'build:release-graph' which produces a directed release graph (JSON) that maps artifacts -> helm chart values -> terraform module versions and includes cryptographic digests and SBOM references; graph is collated from build outputs and attestation metadata - new pipeline step 'validate:manifest' that consumes a release graph and enforces: cosign signature verification (keyless and KMS), SBOM presence, chart values checksum, and compatibility matrix checks for upgrades - small helper library under tools/release-graph that normalizes artifact metadata, computes canonical digests, and emits signatures-ready metadata for the signer job - Helm chart linting rules extended to emit a Helm manifest fingerprint (stable ordering) and surface mismatches between chart contents and release graph - Terraform module manifest extraction to include provider pins and a short policy check to prevent unsupported provider upgrades in the same release - CI orchestration changes: the validate step runs before artifact promotion and blocks publishing to the release bucket on failure - added an automated delta-rollback manifest generator which creates a minimal rollback plan from the release graph when a canary fails Why this is different: existing pipelines stamp artifacts and upload indexes, but we need a top-level release graph that can be consumed by Private control-plane for deterministic rollouts and by on-prem installers to reconcile manifests during upgrades. This PR focuses on making the release artifact a first-class, verifiable graph rather than loose indexes. Checklist: - [x] release-graph generator implemented - [x] manifest validator implemented and integrated into the CI workflow - [x] cosign verification (keyless and KMS) wired into validator - [x] Helm fingerprinting integrated - [x] Terraform module extraction implemented - [x] unit tests for tools/release-graph - [x] e2e run in staging that simulates signed artifact promotion - [x] added docs for ops on how to interpret the release graph Changes included (high level): tools/release-graph/* (new library and CLI), ci/pipeline/build-release-graph.yaml (new job), ci/pipeline/validate-manifest.yaml (new job), charts/private-installer/patches/manifest-fingerprint.yaml (helper), terraform/modules/release-metadata/README.md (docs), scripts/delta-rollback/gen-rollback.sh (new helper), tests/integration/release-graph-e2e (e2e harness). Testing and verification: - unit coverage for release-graph normalization functions - integration test that generates a release graph from staged artifacts, signs them with a test KMS key, then runs validator (CI job uses real cosign verification in staging) - staging rollout run that exercised the delta-rollback generator after a simulated canary failure - CI history shows two failed validator runs during early iteration (missing SBOMs) which were fixed by the chart/lint rule update Rollout plan: - merged to main and enabled in staging for one week; manual gating for production release (ops approval required) - enable automatic blocking on validator failures for subsequent releases; until then validator failures are opt-in via release-note flag Migration notes: - existing release index consumers must be updated to prefer release-graph.json; a small compatibility shim is provided under tools/release-graph/bin/compatify - operators must ensure cosign public key or KMS access is available in the Private control-plane to verify signatures Security considerations: - cosign key usage requires KMS scopes; the validator supports both keyless (OIDC) verification and KMS-backed verification; by default validator rejects unsigned artifacts - SBOM presence is mandatory for private installer releases; policy can be relaxed via a per-customer policy file if needed Documentation: - docs/private-installer/release-graph.md added explaining format and how to use the delta-rollback generator - ops playbook updated with steps to manually validate a release graph Known limitations & follow-ups: - release-graph currently serializes all digests in SHA256; we will add multi-algo support (ENG-6780 follow-up) - we do not yet have an automated remediation when validator fails (ENG-6723 will track a retry/auto-fix job) Related: ENG-6723 (release graph spec), ENG-6780 (multi-algo hashing), SUP-3048 (support ticket where customer asked for deterministic rollback plans).

title: Introduce lightweight tracepoint sampler and batch-pressure microbench for scheduler visibility
preview:
Motivation: We need a low-overhead way to correlate scheduler decisions, KV-cache hot prefixes, and continuous-batching pressure in production-like workloads. Existing NVTX and perfetto exports are useful but either too heavy or too coarse for iterative scheduler tuning. This PR introduces a lightweight tracepoint sampler plus a compact microbench harness that reproduces batch-pressure scenarios seen in customer workloads.

What this PR does: 1) Introduces a TracepointSampler module (C++): periodic, low-payload tracepoints emitted around scheduler windowing and key KV-cache touchpoints. Sampling is rate-limited and context-tagged so it is safe to enable in Dedicated/Private pockets for short experiments. 2) Adds a BatchPressureMicrobench: a small harness that simulates mixed arrival patterns (burst+poisson), tracks batch fill/evict cycles, and reports per-step metrics (queue depth, avg-batch-size, scheduler-latency). 3) Adds a lightweight exporter template that converts sampler output to a compact CSV/JSON line format and a dashboard YAML for the perf observability stack. 4) Provides unit and integration tests for correctness and a CI bench job to run the microbench in controlled runners.

Design notes: - TracepointSampler emits an inline 64-bit tag plus epoch counter; it avoids heavy NVTX payloads to keep overhead < 0.2% in our microbench. - The sampler can be configured by percent-sampling or deterministic-epoch windows; configuration lives under runtime.conf under [tracepoint_sampler]. - The bench harness focuses on reproducing scheduler backpressure (when batches are delayed waiting for KV-cache or kernel queues). It is intentionally small so it can be run in CI and locally.

Benchmarks: Local runs on an r5-like simulated env show: - 0.12% median runtime overhead with sampler enabled at 1% sampling. - Microbench reproduces a clear inflection when batch-timeouts exceed 6ms and shows scheduler queue growth matching production traces. Included CSV outputs for two representative runs in bench/outputs/*.csv.

Testing & CI: - Added unit tests for sampler tagging and epoch wrap-around. - Added a bench CI job (ci/bench/bench-runner.yml) that executes microbench in a small container; results are archived as artifacts. CI initially failed due to a missing clock dependency in the test harness; fixed in second commit.

Migration / Rollout: This is a safe, opt-in instrumentation path. Default sampling is disabled in production configs. We recommend: 1) enable 0.1% sampling on a canary Dedicated node for 24 hours, 2) collect artifacts and load into the dashboard template, 3) iterate sampling/window params and run microbench locally to validate hypotheses.

Follow-ups: - Wire sampler output to the existing perf-trace ingestion pipeline (ENG-1185). - Add a lightweight JS/TS helper to download and visualise CSVs in the console (ENG-1190).

Related: builds on prior instrumentation work but intentionally avoids heavy NVTX payloads and Perfetto dependency so we can run in constrained CI nodes. See related PRs: ENG-93547-style batching probes and ENG-93421 (kv-cache sim) for context.

title: Introduce jurisdiction-aware assertion proxy, KMS consent router, and audit enrichment shim
preview: Motivation: customers with mixed-residency fleets asked for a deterministic place to evaluate operator/service jurisdiction assertions so KMS usage and audit provenance can be routed safely without sprinkling logic across services. This PR introduces an assertion proxy (service: residency-assertor) that normalizes incoming claims, evaluates configured jurisdiction policies, and emits a compact proof token used by downstream KMS routing and audit enrichment. It also adds a KMS Consent Router to pick an appropriate CMK/region and a lightweight audit-enrichment shim that attaches pseudonymous residency and proof metadata to audit events. Implementation summary: 1) residency-assertor service (new binary) with a small policy DSL converted into OPA-compatible JSON; 2) kms-consent-router middleware integrated into the existing kms-proxy client path with a decision cache and TTL; 3) audit-enricher sidecar that subscribes to commit log and augments events with residency_proof and audit_cohort tags; 4) feature flags and rollout controls (config path: /deploy/feature-flags/jurisdiction_assertion.json) and a canary rollout plan. Security and compliance notes: decisions compute a minimal proof (HMAC-signed) containing issuer, asserted_region, policy_id and exp; the HMAC key is stored in KMS and rotated via existing rotation pipeline; audit enrichment strips any direct tenant-identifiers and emits cohort hashes to maintain investigatability without exposing PII. Backwards compatibility: all services fall back to existing routing when the feature flag is off or residency-assertor returns no-decision. Performance: decision cache and async enrichment keep tail latency impact <5ms p95 on request path in our local benchmarks. Checklist: - [x] Policy DSL parser and unit tests - [x] kms-consent-router integrated and e2e tests - [x] audit-enricher implemented with audit schema migration + migration test harness - [x] Feature flag gating and canary rollout manifest - [x] Documentation: runbook + threat model note and operator guide - [x] Added telemetry: rbac_decisionlatencies, kms_routing_hits, audit_enrich_failures Rollout plan: staged canary to 5% tenants in region-us then 20% then global over two weeks; automated rollback triggers on >1% enrichment failures or >10% request-latency degradation. text: Motivation: customers with mixed-residency fleets asked for a deterministic place to evaluate operator/service jurisdiction assertions so KMS usage and audit provenance can be routed safely without sprinkling logic across services. This PR introduces an assertion proxy (service: residency-assertor) that normalizes incoming claims, evaluates configured jurisdiction policies, and emits a compact proof token used by downstream KMS routing and audit enrichment. It also adds a KMS Consent Router to pick an appropriate CMK/region and a lightweight audit-enrichment shim that attaches pseudonymous residency and proof metadata to audit events. Implementation summary: 1) residency-assertor service (new binary) with a small policy DSL converted into OPA-compatible JSON; 2) kms-consent-router middleware integrated into the existing kms-proxy client path with a decision cache and TTL; 3) audit-enricher sidecar that subscribes to commit log and augments events with residency_proof and audit_cohort tags; 4) feature flags and rollout controls (config path: /deploy/feature-flags/jurisdiction_assertion.json) and a canary rollout plan. Security and compliance notes: decisions compute a minimal proof (HMAC-signed) containing issuer, asserted_region, policy_id and exp; the HMAC key is stored in KMS and rotated via existing rotation pipeline; audit enrichment strips any direct tenant-identifiers and emits cohort hashes to maintain investigatability without exposing PII. Backwards compatibility: all services fall back to existing routing when the feature flag is off or residency-assertor returns no-decision. Performance: decision cache and async enrichment keep tail latency impact <5ms p95 on request path in our local benchmarks. title: heterogeneous-attention-fusion-with-staged-scheduler-and-continuous-batching-support
preview: Motivation: reduce p99 latency regression for mixed-sequence workloads and improve throughput for production chat and retrieval runtimes by selecting optimal attention kernels at runtime and enabling continuous batching without sacrificing KV cache hit stability. Changes: Introduces a heterogeneous attention selection layer (HeteroAttn) that picks between a low-latency microkernel and a high-throughput Flash-like kernel based on sequence length heuristics and current queueing pressure; adds a staged scheduler that enforces a short-term reservation window for latency-sensitive requests and a batch-aggregation window for throughput requests; implements a cooperative KV cache eviction policy (staged LRU) that works with continuous batching to avoid repeated cache thrash during rollups; adds fused rotary/rope offset precomputation paths that avoid memcpy for common rotary patterns; integrates fp16 accumulation path with optional fp32 fallback for edge-case stability; exposes runtime knobs: ATTEN_SELECT_WINDOW_MS, KV_STAGED_WINDOW, CBATCH_MAX_WAIT_MS for tuned rollouts. Testing and validation: microbenchmarked on c6i-gpu clusters and internal perf-lab; added synthetic mixed-sequence workload harness and end-to-end chat replay traces. Results: median latency improved 6-9% on mid-sized seqs, p99 reduced up to 22% for mixed workloads, throughput increased 12% on long-sequence tails while KV miss rate decreased 18% under continuous batching. Rollout plan: staged canary on dedicated pool alpha -> 50% traffic after 72h, automatic rollback on p99 regressions >10% or error rate increase >0.5%. Backwards compatibility: no API changes. Known limitations: rare fp16 accumulation differences at extreme sequence lengths (documented and guarded by runtime flag), scheduler parameters tuned for our standard clusters; customers on small on-prem may require adjustments. Checklist: - [x] Benchmarks added to perf-lab harness - [x] Unit + integration tests for scheduler and KV policy - [x] Feature-flagged rollout - [x] Docs entry in runtime tuning guide - [x] Telemetry events for selection decisions

title: introduce-auth-tactician-ttl-prefetch-and-pullstream-refactor
preview: Context: Cross-SDK follow-up to earlier session/lease work — introduces an "auth tactician" component that centralizes token TTL awareness and a small prefetch policy to avoid tail latency for streamed pull/push APIs. Motivation: customers saw intermittent ~200-400ms spikes when long-running streams triggered last-second token refreshes; retry/backoff logic masked this but increased request churn and observable noise. Goal: reduce tail latency by proactively warming credentials within a TTL window, provide a small, well-documented opt-in prefetch policy across Python/TS/Go SDKs, and clean up PullStream ergonomics so the language SDKs expose a common surface for cancellable/asynchronous consumption. What I changed: 1) auth/tactician: new minimal stateful component (Python: redwood_sdk.session.tactician, TS: auth/tactician.ts, Go: session/tactician.go) that tracks token TTLs, schedules a single prefetch per session when TTL < configured window, and exposes hooks for the SDK session factories to opt into warm-up; 2) prefetch policy: defaults to disabled for backwards compatibility, opt-in via SDK session config as prefetch_window_ms and prefetch_enabled; adds telemetry events (tactician.prefetch.scheduled, tactician.prefetch.success, tactician.prefetch.error); 3) PullStream refactor: unified pullstream interface name changes (Python: PullAsync -> PullStreamAsync, TS: PullStream class tidy, Go: PullIterator improvements) and clarified cancellation semantics in all three SDKs; 4) retries interplay: small change to retry predicate so a refresh in-flight counts as a non-retryable transient state for a single attempt to avoid double-refresh races; 5) docs and examples: migration guide, prefetch demo, and updated version notes for Python/TS/Go SDK releases. Compatibility notes: all changes are opt-in and default behavior is preserved; SDK minor bumps included to expose the new options (py 2.7.0, ts 1.18.0, go 1.5.0). Testing: unit tests around tactician scheduling and backoff, integration tests that run a long-lived PullStream while forcing token TTL expiry, and end-to-end scenario against a staging control-plane that simulates delayed token issuance. Rollout plan: feature flagged server-side and SDK flags default off; enable in canary customers after 2 weeks smoke. Checklist: [x] add tactician implementations across three SDKs [x] add telemetry and docs [x] add integration tests [x] add version bumps and changelogs [x] run cross-SDK regression job

title: Tokenwise scale-lowering and ring-buffer collation for quantized kernels
preview:
Summary: Adds a tokenwise scale-lowering compiler pass and a ring-buffer collation stage in the runtime to reduce per-token quant/dequant churn and to coalesce KV cache writes from continuous-batching. Motivation: some workloads with short prefixes and frequent small continuations were suffering from per-token scale conversions and tight kernel launches that prevented microbatch coalescing on INT8 and FP8 execution paths. This PR targets the hot path by lowering scale operations earlier and grouping adjacent KV writes into a ring-backed collator so the GPU-side kernel sees larger contiguous work items without changing model numerics.

What changed (high-level):
- New compiler pass runtime/quant/scale_lowering.cc that detects consecutive token-scale operations and emits a lowered tokenwise scale applied as a fused kernel input where safe. The pass is careful to preserve per-head scaling semantics and falls back to explicit dequant when quantization boundaries are ambiguous.
- Ring-buffer collation runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.cc which accumulates KV cache writes from the continuous-batching queue and emits coalesced write batches to the kernel backend. Collation is implemented lock-free per-GPU-stream and supports configurable watermark/latency tradeoffs.
- Cache eviction hook runtime/cache/evictor.h to ensure collated segments respect existing per-shard eviction windows and do not hold stale KV pointers across rollouts.
- Microbench harness bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py and a small integration test tests/quant/scale_lowering_test.cc that validate numerical parity against the baseline within tolerated quantization noise.
- Docs: docs/perf/quant-collation.md describing knobs, feature flags, and recommended settings for Dedicated and Private deployments.

Commits (summary):
- feat(quant): introduce tokenwise scale-lowering pass (Marina Alvarez)
- perf(scheduler): add ring buffer collator and watermark policy (Marina Alvarez)
- fix(tests): stabilize scale-lowering unit tests and edge-case fallbacks (Marina Alvarez)
- bench: add token collation microbench and CI benchmark job (Marina Alvarez)
- docs: usage and rollout guide for quant collation (Marina Alvarez)

Files touched (representative):
- runtime/quant/scale_lowering.cc, runtime/quant/scale_lowering.h
- runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.cc, runtime/scheduler/ring_collator.h
- runtime/cache/evictor.h
- bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py
- tests/quant/scale_lowering_test.cc
- docs/perf/quant-collation.md

Design notes and tradeoffs:
- Numeric safety: the scale-lowering pass applies a conservative analysis to ensure per-head and per-token scale semantics are preserved. Where the analysis is inconclusive (eg. custom layer-scale hooks or non-standard quant tables), the pass emits an explicit dequant/requant path to preserve correctness. The feature is gated by runtime.quant.scale_lowering and runtime.quant.enable_collation flags.
- Latency vs throughput: ring collator uses a watermark delay (default 1.5ms) to collect contiguous KV writes; on low-concurrency workloads customers can reduce or disable collation to prioritize single-request latency.
- Memory: ring-buffer allocates an ephemeral per-stream buffer sized by max_batch_tokens which defaults to 8k tokens per GPU. This is configurable and documented.

Benchmarks:
- Microbench on A100 (seq_len 2048, batch 8, generation 128): throughput +15% for INT8 end-to-end token/s compared to baseline quant path; median tail latency (p95) increased <2% with default watermark.
- Real workload sim (chat-like prefixes with many small continuations): median tokens/sec +18% and average GPU kernel launches reduced by ~22%. Detailed bench outputs attached in PR artifacts.

Review conversation (high level):
- Daniel Kim (review): "Nice win. Can you add a small test covering per-head scales and the mixed-precision fallback? Also please verify this doesn't regress KV streaming on Dedicated low-memory instances."
- Author reply: "Added per-head unit test and an explicit Dedicated config run in CI. Evictor hook added to avoid stale pointer windows."
- Sofia Nguyen (review): "Please document the feature flags and safe rollout steps; add a metrics counter for collated_batches and collated_tokens."
- Author reply: "Docs added and two counters (quant.collation.batches, quant.collation.tokens) exposed to console and Prometheus."
- Ethan Park (approval): "Bench numbers look solid; tests are green. Recommend squash-merge after CI smoke passes."

CI and validation:
- Unit tests: pass
- Integration (scale path): pass
- Bench job: artifacts show throughput improvements; bench job marked as informational in CI (not gating).
- Lint/format: pass

Rollout plan:
1) Merge behind feature flag off by default.
2) Canary on small Dedicated pool (internal tag: canary-quant-collation) with metrics for collated_batches and kernel_launch_count.
3) Gradual ramp with health checks for tail latency and model-quality regressions (Linear ENG-9273 monitors).

Related: Linear ENG-9273 (quant perf initiative), ENG-9410 (kv cache coherency), SUP-1891 (customer report of small-continuation latency).

Notes for maintainers:
- If future work expands the pass to span inter-layer fusions, ensure the analysis preserves custom kernel hooks (eg. fused FusedFFN variants).
- The ring collator intentionally avoids reordering across tokens belonging to different request IDs; do not change that invariant without consensus.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged after green CI and two approvals.

If you want to test locally: see docs/perf/quant-collation.md and run bench/quant/token_collation_bench.py with --gpu-streams and --watermark-ms to reproduce microbench numbers.

title: Ephemeral runbook cache and idempotent rollforward handler for safe post-incident triage
preview: Context and motivation: On high-noise incidents the incident-bot runbook lookups and remediation orchestration were adding 150-300ms per request and occasionally executing non-idempotent handlers twice when a race in the triage state machine retried a step. This PR introduces a bounded ephemeral runbook cache (in-memory with TTL and stale-while-revalidate) to reduce lookup latency, and a 'rollforward' handler wrapper that enforces idempotency and safe retries for automated remediation actions. Goals: 1) reduce median oncall lookup latency under load, 2) prevent double-applies of non-idempotent remediation steps, 3) preserve existing runbook semantics and allow opt-out via annotation. Changes summary: - runbook cache: lightweight LRU with 1s default TTL, 5s stale-while-revalidate, metrics and per-team tunables - rollforward handler: decorator that records ephemeral execution tokens in the triage transaction store and rejects duplicates or replays; supports compensating actions and safe-noop return codes - migrations: small schema bump to triage transactions to add an executions map (backfilled lazily) - safety: feature flag off by default for first week, auto-fallback to existing behavior on store errors - telemetry: tracing spans, histogram of cache hit/miss, counter for suppressed duplicate executions Why this approach: persistent caching at the platform level would add operational complexity and cross-tenant leak risk. An ephemeral per-instance cache with clear TTLs gives the latency wins while remaining safe for multi-tenant deployments. The rollforward decorator centralizes idempotency logic so individual runbook steps do not need bespoke guards. Backwards compatibility: runbooks without the new annotation keep current semantics. Opt-in: annotate runbook step with "idempotent: true" to enable the stronger protections. Rollout plan: feature-flag gated, dogfood enabled for SRE, staged rollout to 20% of instances on 2025-11-07, monitor cache hit ratio and suppressed-exec counts, ramp to 100% after 72h if no anomalies. Related work: follow-up will add persistent per-team cache and richer compensator registry (ENG-4899). || Commits summary: - 64a2b3d: initial runbook cache implementation and unit tests - 7f1c8b2: add stale-while-revalidate + metrics and config knobs - b2d98f1: rollforward handler, decorator API, and integration tests - c3e9f34: triage transaction schema bump and migration shim - d11a7e0: safety feature flag + toggles in ops console - e4f5a90: docs and runbook examples; update changelog || Files changed (high level): ["pkg/runbook/cache.go", "pkg/runbook/handler_rollforward.go", "pkg/triage/transaction_store.go", "migrations/20251103_add_exec_map.sql", "internal/flags/feature_flags.go", "docs/runbooks/idempotency.md", "pkg/runbook/cache_test.go"] || Small illustrative snippet (cache constructor): 'func NewEphemeralCache(maxEntries int, ttl time.Duration, stale time.Duration) *Cache { // LRU + TTL + stale-while-revalidate }' || Tests and CI: unit tests added for cache eviction, stale revalidate, rollforward duplicate suppression, and basic integration between triage store and handler. Local benchmarks show median lookup latency drop from 260ms to 60ms for repeated lookups under synthetic load. CI history: initial run failed 'integration-test:triage' due to a flaky test around transaction ordering; test was hardened and rerun, full CI passed on second run. || Review conversation (selected highlights): "Arjun Patel: Overall looks good, I like the simplicity of the cache. title: kv-prefetch-aware-batching-with-sparse-scratch-allocator
preview: Context: In production workloads with long chat histories the KV cache dominates transient GPU workspace allocations and forces arena growth during batching spikes. Motivation: Reduce peak GPU footprint while preserving latency SLOs and steady-state throughput. Summary: Introduces a KV-prefetch hotset buffer and a sparse scratch allocator to avoid inflating the global arena during continuous batching. The hotset prefetcher tracks request-level KV hotness and pre-populates a compact per-device hotset capable of serving the majority of cache hits without expanding the scratch arena. The sparse scratch allocator exposes a lightweight free-list for short-lived activation fragments and returns large contiguous ranges back to the arena quickly, reducing fragmentation. Key changes: 1) runtime/kv: prefetch_hotset component with heuristics for prefix/predictive prefetch and bounded memory cap. 2) runtime/allocator: sparse_scratch_allocator that coexists with existing arena and supports deferred materialization for checkpointed activations. 3) scheduler: integrate prefetch decisions into continuous_batcher so requests in the same batch can share prefetched KV and avoid duplicate allocations. 4) observability: new metrics for kv_hotset_hit_ratio, scratch_frag_pct, and prefetch_evictions, plus a dashboard. Results: in internal LLaMA-2-13B quantized chat trace we saw peak GPU RSS drop ~18% (24.6GB -> 20.1GB), 95th latency unchanged within noise, throughput +3% under steady batching, and per-token cost reduction ~12%. Compatibility: falls back to existing arena+kv behavior when hotset cap is exceeded or prefetch disabled. Rollout: feature-flagged, opt-in per-customer; staged rollout with smoke tests and perf canary on dedicated pools. Checklist: [x] unit tests, [x] integration tests, [x] perf regression run, [x] docs, [x] observability. Related: ENG-863 describes requirements for KV costing and ENG-874 is follow-up for multi-GPU hotset federated eviction.

title: introduce-tenant-kms-binding-enforcer-and-audit-linker
preview: Motivation: We need a stronger guardrail that prevents cross-tenant KMS usage in multi-tenant Dedicated/Private deployments while preserving operator ergonomics and auditability. This PR implements a runtime enforcer that ensures KMS key bindings are validated against the request's tenant/namespace claims before allowing encrypt/decrypt/sign operations. It also introduces an audit linker service that enriches existing audit events with KMS provenance (key id, provider, binding-check-result) and a correlation id that ties application requests to KMS calls for post-incident investigation. Design summary: (1) policy-operator plugin that evaluates declared kms.bindings for a tenant namespace and caches validated bindings in a short-lived policy store; (2) request-time gate in the kms-proxy that checks the caller's tenant claim against the policy store and denies calls that violate bindings; (3) audit-linker sidecar that subscribes to internal audit-stream, joins KMS provenance, and emits correlated audit events to the audit-aggregator with a new field audit.kms_correlation_id; (4) feature-flagged rollout behind operator.feature.flags. This description includes a small checklist and rollout plan. Checklist: - [x] policy schema addition and unit tests - [x] kms-proxy binding checks and integration tests - [x] audit-linker implementation and end-to-end tests - [x] migration to grace period for namespaces with no bindings - [x] docs for admin operators and Linear tickets linked. Testing: unit tests for policy compiler, e2e harness using local kms-mock, chaos run for denial scenarios, and load test to ensure latency added is < 3ms p50 for kms-proxy path. Rollout: staged canary (10% traffic) for 4h, 50% for 12h, 100% for 24h; monitor audit link failures, KMS-deny rates, and latency SLOs. Backout: flip feature flag off to revert enforcement; audit-linker safe-mode preserves original audit stream when disabled. Related: ENG-3421 implements the policy schema; SEC-78 is the security review. Links: linear/ENG-3421, security/SEC-78. Notes: No public API changes. Operators will need to declare kms.bindings for existing namespaces or opt into a grace period to avoid sudden denials.

title: Add standardized overload error codes + JSON payload (Hosted API)
preview:
Context: During overload we currently surface a mix of upstream timeouts, generic 503s, and ad-hoc JSON bodies depending on which hop rejects the request (gateway vs routing vs runtime admission). That makes client behavior inconsistent and increases retry storms (clients retry immediately on timeouts/unknown 5xx). This PR introduces a single canonical overload error response struct and a stable error-code taxonomy for Hosted API overload/load-shedding. Goal is to make overload failures explicit, machine-parseable, and consistent across the request path while remaining backwards compatible (additive fields only). This aligns with ADR-014 (Hosted overload error taxonomy) and sets up follow-on work in the gateway middleware + routing fallback (ENG-4823/ENG-4824).

What changed: 1) Add `OverloadErrorResponse` (shared API error type) with stable fields: `error.type=overload_error`, `error.code` (stable), `error.message` (customer-safe), `error.retryable` (bool), `error.retry_after_ms` (optional), `error.request_id` (correlation id), `error.details` (optional, bounded). 2) Add mapping from internal reasons (gateway/routing/runtime) to stable `OverloadErrorCode` values. We explicitly avoid leaking internal component names and ensure codes don’t change as thresholds/tuning changes. 3) Standardize HTTP status mapping: 429 for tenant/tier admission limits (quota/concurrency/rate-limits), 503 for platform capacity / downstream saturation / brownouts. Status mapping is determined by the stable code, not by the caller. 4) Add unit tests + golden JSON snapshots for response payloads and headers. 5) Add doc-comments / internal “client retry contract” block to guide SDK authors: respect `Retry-After` header when present, use exponential backoff + jitter, do not retry non-idempotent requests without idempotency keys.

Stable error codes added (initial set): - `overload_tenant_concurrency_limit` (429) - `overload_tenant_rate_limit` (429) - `overload_tier_capacity` (503) - `overload_region_capacity` (503) - `overload_runtime_queue_saturated` (503) - `overload_runtime_memory_pressure` (503) - `overload_dependency_unhealthy` (503)

Example response (503): `{ "error": { "type": "overload_error", "code": "overload_region_capacity", "message": "Service is temporarily overloaded. Please retry after a short delay.", "retryable": true, "retry_after_ms": 1200, "request_id": "req_01H..." } }` and `Retry-After: 1` (seconds). Example response (429) uses the same body shape with a 429 status.

Compatibility: This is additive to existing error JSON (we keep `error.message` semantics and don’t remove existing top-level keys). Callers that don’t understand the new fields will continue to parse `message`. Follow-up PRs will adopt this struct in the gateway admission middleware and routing fallback paths so we stop emitting legacy overload bodies.

Notes: - `error.details` is intentionally restricted to low-cardinality values (e.g., `policy_id`, `limit_type`) to avoid leaking sensitive info and avoid observability cardinality blowups. - `request_id` is sourced from existing request context; if missing we generate one at the edge (existing behavior).

Release notes (internal): Hosted API overload errors now include stable `error.code` values and consistent JSON payload shape; clients should treat these as retryable only when `retryable=true` and should honor `Retry-After` when present.

Refs: ADR-014 Hosted overload error taxonomy; Linear ENG-4822.

title: Introduce simultaneous prefetch stress scheduler and OOM spectra reporter
preview:
Motivation: We observed correlated OOMs and long-tail latency spikes when many concurrent inferences attempted identical prefix prefetching/pinning at the same time. Small-scale reproductions masked the issue because they serialized prefetch. This PR introduces a synthetic scheduler that reproduces simultaneous prefetch pressure at scale and a compact OOM 'spectra' reporter that summarizes allocation patterns leading to crashes.

What this change does: Adds a new workload runner (sim-prefetch-stressor) that can spawn thousands of virtual sessions with configurable prefetch windows, KV-cache priming patterns, and stagger jitter; the runner can optionally inject hold-until-commit semantics to simulate long-lived memory pinning. It also adds oom-spectra-reporter, a post-process tool that consumes core dumps/heap snapshots and produces an epochal histogram of allocation-site families, peak-resident working set ranges, and a small tokenized summary suitable for attaching to incidents.

Design notes: The stress scheduler is deterministic by default (seeded RNG) to allow reproducible experiments. It supports two scheduling modes: burst-synchronous (many sessions issue prefetch at the same microsecond) and windowed-overlap (configurable overlap percentage across sessions). The runner coordinates with our tracer to capture per-kernel memory high-water marks and kernel call graphs without instrumenting the runtime. The OOM spectra reporter maps back high-water marks to the synthetic workload timeline so we can attribute which phase caused the spectral spike.

How to run locally: ./bin/run-sim-prefetch --sessions 2000 --prefetch-window 128 --overlap 0.85 --hold-until-commit --trace-output /tmp/trace.pb
Then: ./tools/oom-spectra-reporter --trace /tmp/trace.pb --snapshots ./snapshots/ --out report.json

Testing and validation: The PR includes a small cluster job that runs on the benchmark lab CI cluster (k8s-ci-bench) with a 3-node rtx-cluster profile; it produces a p95/p99 latency heatmap, memory high-water timelines, and an OOM attribution CSV. We validated that burst-synchronous mode reproduces the multi-node OOM seen in PROD incident INT-842 and that moving to staggered prefetch reduced concurrent pinned bytes by ~58% in our synthetic test.

Backward compatibility: The runner and reporter are opt-in. No runtime or serving-side defaults are changed. The new tools are added under benchmark-lab tooling and do not change any public APIs.

Rollout and follow-up: After merging, we will schedule two canary runs against the perf-canary cluster (low-traffic) and integrate the OOM spectra output into incident-bot to auto-attach compact crash attribution for future incidents.

Related: ENG-1893, INT-842, previous exploratory WIP pr-314877 (context only).

title: Add post-provision smoke canary for Dedicated pools
preview:
Context: As part of the Provision-to-ready automation sprint, we want a workload-realistic validation step after a GPU node (or pool expansion) is marked provisioned but before we declare it Ready for inference workloads. The existing post-provision checks cover driver parity + NCCL smoke + kubelet join, but we were still missing a fast end-to-end “serving path” signal (runtime boot, model load, tokenization, request/streaming, basic latency sanity). This PR adds a lightweight post-provision smoke/perf canary for Dedicated pools. It runs as a short-lived Job in the target cluster/pool, executes a small fixed prompt set against the local inference endpoint (or local runtime shim in dev), and emits a single structured result that the provisioning pipeline can treat as a gate.

What this does: (1) Adds a new canary mode: post-provision/dedicated_smoke. (2) Provides a Kubernetes Job template + runner that can be invoked by the provisioning orchestrator (via Helm values + CLI flags). (3) Implements a minimal inference check: warm-up request, then N requests (streaming optional) with token-count normalization and a bounded timeout. (4) Records result artifacts (JSON) and emits metrics: pass/fail, latency p50/p95, tokens/sec, and failure reason category. (5) Adds guardrails for metric cardinality (no per-node labels; only pool_id, region, gpu_type, image_channel, canary_type). (6) Documents how to run locally and how the provisioning gate should consume results.

Key behaviors/constraints: The canary is designed to complete within ~3 minutes in the common case and never exceed 8 minutes (hard deadline) to avoid ballooning time-to-ready. Failures are categorized to be actionable (MODEL_LOAD, RUNTIME_BOOT, REQUEST_TIMEOUT, HTTP_5XX, AUTHZ, OUTPUT_INVALID, PERF_REGRESSION). The perf thresholds are intentionally loose for initial rollout; we only fail hard on catastrophic perf (tokens/sec below floor) or timeouts, and we emit a “warn-only” flag for borderline cases so SRE can tune without paging.

Rollout plan: This is gated behind a feature flag in the provisioning system (to be wired in control plane) and defaults to disabled. Initial enablement targeted at us-east / A100 Dedicated pools only (staged rollout).

Testing: Unit tests for result classification + JSON schema; integration test in kind using a stubbed inference endpoint; helm template tests; CI includes a smoke run of the runner with a mocked endpoint.

Follow-ups: (a) Wire result upload pointer into fleet-readiness-service validation artifacts (tracked in ENG-4822/PR in redwood repo). (b) Tune perf thresholds per GPU type once we have baseline data. (c) Add a small “embedding-only” canary variant for pools that don’t serve chat.

Risk: Low/medium. Primary risk is false negatives due to transient cold-start/model pull delays; mitigated via warm-up, bounded retries, and explicit classification. Also added conservative timeouts and backoff to avoid hanging provisioning.

title: Record validation artifacts and failure reasons in Fleet Readiness service
preview: Context: Provision-to-ready automation needs actionable failure modes and durable artifacts (logs, summaries) per node before we can safely gate nodes from joining serving pools. Today the orchestrator/agent emits logs, but we lose structured per-step results once a node is recycled, and operators have to grep raw logs to understand why a node is stuck in Validating. This PR adds persistence for validation step results and a structured failure reason field to the Fleet Readiness service, with an API to fetch the latest results for a node and an API-friendly summary intended for internal tooling (ops console/runbooks). Scope: store step name, start/end timestamps, duration_ms, status, failure_reason_code/message, and an artifact pointer (URI + checksum) to bundled logs uploaded by the validation agent. Also adds minimal telemetry for write/read success and payload sizes (no per-node high-cardinality tags). Out of scope: changing the canonical readiness state machine (see ADR-017) or adding UI surfaces; those are in follow-on PRs. Rollout: gated behind a server-side config flag `readiness.persist_validation_results` default OFF; staged enablement planned with SRE after initial data quality checks. Security/PII: artifacts are pointers only; we do not store raw log bodies in Postgres, and artifact URIs are restricted to internal buckets and require service authz to read.
text: Context: Provision-to-ready automation needs actionable failure modes and durable artifacts (logs, summaries) per node before we can safely gate nodes from joining serving pools. Today the orchestrator/agent emits logs, but we lose structured per-step results once a node is recycled, and operators have to grep raw logs to understand why a node is stuck in Validating. This PR adds persistence for validation step results and a structured failure reason field to the Fleet Readiness service, with an API to fetch the latest results for a node and an API-friendly summary intended for internal tooling (ops console/runbooks). Scope: store step name, start/end timestamps, duration_ms, status, failure_reason_code/message, and an artifact pointer (URI + checksum) to bundled logs uploaded by the validation agent. Also adds minimal telemetry for write/read success and payload sizes (no per-node high-cardinality tags). Out of scope: changing the canonical readiness state machine (see ADR-017) or adding UI surfaces; those are in follow-on PRs. Rollout: gated behind a server-side config flag `readiness.persist_validation_results` default OFF; staged enablement planned with SRE after initial data quality checks. Security/PII: artifacts are pointers only; we do not store raw log bodies in Postgres, and artifact URIs are restricted to internal buckets and require service authz to read.

title: stream-slice-attn-kernel-with-rope-interleaving-and-proactive-kv-warmup
preview:
Summary: Introduces a new stream-slice attention microkernel and scheduler integration that interleaves rope offsets at dispatch time and proactively warms KV cache for streaming/continuous-batching workloads.

Motivation: short, variable-length streams suffer high p99 first-token latency because attention kernels and KV caches are optimized for batched, fixed-length inference. This PR adds a slice-oriented kernel that (1) fuses QK scaling, rope application, and numeric-stable softmax into a single path for short sequences, (2) interleaves rope/rope-offset computation to avoid re-applying rotary per-slice, and (3) exposes a proactive KV warmup API used by the continuous-batcher to prefetch likely KV ranges into the slab before tokens arrive.

Checklist:
- [x] implement stream-slice microkernel (CUDA + CPU fallback)
- [x] scheduler hook for proactive KV warmup and warmup window heuristics
- [x] unit tests for rope interleave correctness and numeric parity
- [x] end-to-end bench harness and perf tests
- [x] CI perf job integration and documentation notes

Changes and rationale: The new kernel (runtime/attn/stream_slice_kernel.cu) uses a narrow-path optimized for sequence lengths <= 64 where branch overhead and memory fetch patterns differ from long-sequence kernels. To avoid repeated rotary arithmetics, rope offset computation is performed once per slice group and interleaved into the fused qk path; this reduces arithmetic and memory churn when the same rotary base is reused across interleaved minibatches. We also fuse softmax+dropout+scaling for the short-path to reduce kernel launches and improve numerical stability using a compensated accumulator in fp16/amp mode.

Scheduler integration: The continuous-batcher now emits a warmup hint when it sees a stream with rising token-rate, and the prefetcher reserves a small contiguous range of KV slab pages and issues background reads so the attention kernel finds warmed pages on first access.

Eviction policy: Added a lightweight warmup-aware eviction bump that keeps pages marked "recently-warmed" slightly higher priority for short windows to avoid immediate eviction during noisy traffic.

Tests and benchmarks:
Microbench:
- Token throughput +12% for 8x32-token batched streams on A100
- End-to-end p50 token latency unchanged, p99 first-token latency reduced by 18% on short sequences (<=64)
- Memory traffic (global load bytes) reduced ~9% in short-paths

Bench harness: added runtime/bench/stream_slice_bench.py and CI perf job runtime/perf/short_stream_job.yaml

Implementation notes:
- Exposed a minimal public API: runtime::attn::request_prefetch(stream_id, warmup_tokens) so higher-level routing/ingress can request warmup.
- Kernel guarded with knob runtime.attn.short_path_enabled (default true for dedicated/private, opt-in for hosted until staged rollout)
- CPU fallback included to preserve correctness on dev machines and CI.

Commits: ["Add stream-slice microkernel and headers", "Wire up scheduler warmup hints and prefetch path", "Add perf harness and short-stream CI job", "Fix parity test for rope interleave with odd seq len", "Tweak eviction bump constants based on bench results"]

Files changed (high-level): ["runtime/attn/stream_slice_kernel.cu", "runtime/attn/stream_slice_kernel.h", "runtime/attn/short_path_dispatch.cc", "runtime/scheduler/warmup_prefetcher.cc", "runtime/scheduler/warmup_prefetcher.h", "runtime/kv/eviction_policy.cc", "runtime/kv/eviction_policy.h", "runtime/kv/kv_slab.h", "runtime/bench/stream_slice_bench.py", "runtime/perf/short_stream_job.yaml", "docs/ops/short_streams.md", "tests/attn/test_stream_slice_parity.py", "tests/attn/test_rope_interleave.py", "build/CMakeLists.txt", "tools/ci/perf/short_stream_perf.yml", "tools/ci/unit/unit.yml"]

Review conversation (summary):
- Marco Diaz: "Can you justify the correctness of the rope interleaving when slices have non-uniform offsets? Need tests for odd-length interleaving." Author: "Added parity test test_rope_interleave.py that covers odd/even offsets and vectorized vs scalar fallback; added comments in header explaining stride math."
- Lina Gomez: "What's the impact on memory fragmentation when warmup pins slab pages?" Author: "Eviction bump is a small multiplier (1.2x) on recency score for warmed pages and it times out after 3s by default; added unit tests to ensure it doesn't starve compaction."
- Ethan Park: "Perf looks promising; can we gate default behavior for hosted customers?" Author: "Yes — short_path_enabled default is off for hosted and feature-flagged so we can rollout via feature gate."

CI and quality:
- Unit tests: all passing (including the new parity tests)
- Integration tests: streaming smoke tests passed
- Perf CI: initial run flaky on one worker due to a noisy host (job retried and passed)
- Linting and build: warnings addressed in subsequent commit

Merge notes: - Squash-merge with authorship preserved. No release blocker.

Rollout plan:
- Stage: enable for Dedicated pools in canary region next week
- Monitor: perf dashboards added (token-p99, kv-miss-rate, eviction-rate)
- Feature flag: enable for hosted after 2 weeks if no regressions

Observed perf numbers and caveats: Results are representative of our internal benchmarks on p4d/a100 clusters; gains are concentrated on short sequences and workloads with frequent stream warmups (chatty clients). For long sequences (>512) the long-path remains the fastest and unchanged. Related: references ENG-1428 (continuous-batching improvements) and ENG-1504 (kv-slab eviction tuning).
