title: Add Private minimum pack: versioned Grafana dashboard bundle (runtime + control plane + infra)
preview: Summary: This PR adds the first versioned Grafana dashboard bundle for the Private Observability Minimum Pack. The dashboards are designed to be shipped as immutable artifacts (checked into this repo) and later installed via `redwood-helm-charts`/`redwood-private-installer` (follow-ups). Scope: 1) Runtime overview dashboard focused on golden signals + inference-specific panels (token throughput, batching, KV cache, GPU utilization). 2) Control plane health dashboard (gateway/auth/policy/config services) with standard service golden signals and dependency panels (DB/redis if present). 3) Infra dashboard for customer cluster basics required to triage most common Private incidents (node pressure, PVC pressure, pod restart loops, scrape status). Design notes / constraints: - Private environments require bounded label cardinality; dashboards avoid templating on high-cardinality labels like raw `route`, `tenant_id`, `request_id`. Where filtering is useful, panels use a small set of safe dimensions (`namespace`, `service`, `cluster`, `instance`), and model/route usage is represented via top-k panels based on recording rules (to be introduced in ENG-19144/PR-18356). - Dashboards are versioned under `dashboards/private/minimum-pack/v1.0.0/` and include stable UIDs prefixed with `rw-private-` to ensure upgrades don’t create duplicates. - Panels include conservative defaults (5m/1h ranges) and annotate ‘expected exporters’ (kube-state-metrics, node-exporter, NVIDIA DCGM exporter) but do not hard-depend on optional exporters (panels degrade gracefully when metrics are missing). What’s included (v1.0.0): - `Runtime / Overview (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-runtime-overview`) - `Control Plane / Health (Private)` (UID: `rw-private-control-plane-health`) - `Private / Cluster Infra` (UID: `rw-private-cluster-infra`) - `Private / Prometheus Scrape Health` (UID: `rw-private-prom-scrape-health`) (small helper dashboard to debug ‘empty panels’ issues) Implementation details: - Adds a bundle manifest (`dashboards/private/minimum-pack/bundle.yaml`) that enumerates dashboards, UIDs, and target folder names for the installer. - Adds lightweight validation tooling in CI (`make dashboards-validate`) to ensure exported JSON is stable, has a UID, and does not embed org-specific datasource IDs. - Standardizes dashboard tags: `redwood`, `private`, `minimum-pack`, and component tag (e.g. `runtime`, `control-plane`, `infra`). Testing: - Ran `make dashboards-validate` locally. - Imported into Grafana 10.4 with a local Prometheus, verified template variables resolve and panels render with a simulated dataset. - Verified dashboards import with no ‘Datasource not found’ errors by using named datasource `Prometheus` and `Loki` placeholders (installer will map). Follow-ups (not in this PR): - Wire into `redwood-helm-charts` values + installer packaging (ENG-19152 / PR-18405). - Add recording rules and alerts referenced by some panels (ENG-19144 / PR-18356). - Add on-prem offline bundle inclusion + checksums/signing (ENG-19154 / PR-18441).

title: Introduce provisioning-rate-fader-and-surge-quench-for-smoother-burst-handling
preview:
Motivation: We observed repeated microspikes in tenant traffic that triggered aggressive warm/pool provisioning which led to oscillation in headroom and higher cost. This PR implements a provisioning rate fader and a surge quench mechanism to smooth capacity onboarding for Dedicated fleets while preserving burst guarantees.

What this PR does (summary): Introduces a rate-fading controller that caps the maximum provisioning throughput (nodes/min and GPUs/min) per tenant/fleet and gradually ramps allocations based on an observed warmup progress signal. Adds a surge_quench policy that temporarily reduces speculative warm preprovisioning when the system detects high churn or repeated short spikes to avoid thrash. Exposes new telemetry and control knobs for operators and a tenant-visible advisory metric for customers using Dedicated.

Key features: 1) provisioning_rate_fader: configurable per-fleet rate limits, backoff on failed attachments, and a linear+sigmoid ramp for steady increases; 2) surge_quench_policy: detects short-lived spike patterns (windowed spike_count + decay) and applies a short suppression of speculative warmups with a jittered cooldown to prevent synchronized retries; 3) warmup_progress metric: a normalized 0..1 progress indicator for each warmlet that feeds the fader so we only count stable warmlets towards headroom; 4) headroom_credits reconciliation: when quench is active we preferentially allocate from pooled credits before spinning new capacity; 5) canary rollout: feature gate + 5% canary across dedicated pools with automatic rollback threshold on latency or allocation failure.

Backward compatibility and config: All behavior is behind the fleet-level feature flag (provisioning_rate_fader=true). Default server config keeps current behavior. New config keys added to dedicated/fleet-config: rate_fader.max_nodes_per_min, rate_fader.max_gpus_per_min, rate_fader.ramp_profile (linear|sigmoid), surge_quench.enabled, surge_quench.spike_window_seconds, surge_quench.suppression_seconds, surge_quench.cooldown_jitter_ms. No API surface changes for customers unless they opt in.

Testing and validation: Unit tests for ramp math and spike detection, integration tests exercising warmlet lifecycle under synthetic spike patterns, e2e canary with k8s-provisioner stub in staging. Load tests show reduction in peak provisioning churn by ~42% and 16% lower short-term overprovision costs in simulated tenant traces.

Checklist: - [x] Design doc reviewed (ENG-4123) - [x] Unit tests added - [x] Integration tests added - [x] Canary plan documented - [x] Telemetry dashboards & alerts added - [x] Feature flag gating in place

Rollout plan: Merge to main -> enable feature flag in canary clusters -> monitor provisioning_rate, warmup_progress, burst_quench_events, allocation_failures for 48h -> increment rollout to 25% then 100% if stable. Rollback if allocation_failures > 0.5% or P95 latency regression > 20% compared to baseline.

Notes: This PR intentionally biases toward reducing noisy short spikes by suppressing speculative warmups; for tenants with strict burst SLAs operators can tune surge_quench thresholds or disable the quench for selected pools.

title: Add audit export permissions + default role mappings (enterprise admin vs auditor)
preview:
Summary
This updates the rbac-policy-bundle to include new permissions required for SIEM-ready audit export configuration + operational visibility. It also updates default role mappings to keep least-privilege separation between enterprise admins (manage exports) and auditors (read-only + status).

Context
The control plane and Console are introducing audit export CRUD + delivery status endpoints behind a beta feature flag. This PR updates the central policy bundle so permissions are standardized across Hosted and Dedicated. Private/on-prem roles are intentionally unchanged for beta.

Linked work
- ENG-5173 (RBAC permissions for export management)
- Control plane endpoints: redwood/pr-28792 (RBAC) + redwood/pr-28766 (config API) (FYI)

What changed
1) New permission set (names + semantics)
- audit_exports.read
  - Allows viewing export configurations (destination type, enabled/disabled, filter settings, backfill window) but not secrets.
- audit_exports.write
  - Allows creating/updating/deleting export configurations, enabling/disabling exports, and modifying filters/backfill controls.
- audit_exports.secrets.rotate
  - Allows rotating credential references (e.g., Splunk HEC token ref, Datadog API key ref, AWS role ARN / external ID ref). Does not grant read access to underlying secret material.
- audit_exports.status.read
  - Allows viewing operational status/health (last_success_at, last_error_at, export_lag_seconds, adapter error counters) surfaced in Console.

Notes on least-privilege
- Secrets are represented as references (vault path / secret manager ID) and never exposed as readable fields via RBAC in this bundle.
- audit_exports.write does not imply audit event read access.
- audit_exports.status.read is separated so auditors/SecOps can monitor health without config write privileges.

2) Default role mapping updates
- enterprise_admin (and legacy admin-equivalent roles)
  - Granted: audit_exports.read, audit_exports.write, audit_exports.secrets.rotate, audit_exports.status.read
- security_auditor
  - Granted: audit_exports.read, audit_exports.status.read
  - Not granted: audit_exports.write, audit_exports.secrets.rotate
- readonly / billing / developer roles
  - No change (no export management permissions added)

3) Policy docs / examples
- Added a short section to the role catalog describing audit export permissions and recommended assignment ("Admin owns configuration; Auditor owns monitoring + evidence"), plus a note that export delivery is at-least-once and downstream dedup is recommended.

Why this approach
- Matches separation-of-duties expectations from Security reviews (config vs oversight).
- Minimizes blast radius if an auditor token is compromised (can’t change destinations/filters or rotate creds).
- Keeps permission granularity aligned with control plane endpoint grouping.

Testing
- bundle lint/validate
- role compilation tests
- snapshot update for default roles

Rollout
- No runtime change; this is a policy bundle update.
- Control plane enforcement remains feature-flagged per tenant allowlist.

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add a dedicated "security_ops" role variant if we see customers wanting status.read without read of config details (currently status.read still requires config metadata for Console UX).
- Revisit permission naming consistency with other integrations (e.g., destinations.*) once API surface stabilizes.

Checklist
- [x] New permissions documented
- [x] Default role mappings updated
- [x] CI green
- [x] Reviewed with Security + Control Plane

Review notes / discussion
Ethan Park (requested changes, 2025-03-22): Can we split status read from config read? Console status page is going to call both endpoints; but conceptually we should keep them separate.
Author (reply): Added audit_exports.status.read as separate permission. Kept audit_exports.read required for config listing/details. status.read is now separate and can be granted independently. (Control plane already checks both as needed.)

Marcus Lin (requested changes, 2025-03-23): Please ensure auditors cannot rotate credentials and cannot enable/disable exports; also confirm no secret material is in any readable resource fields.
Author (reply): Confirmed. Rotation is gated by audit_exports.secrets.rotate. Enable/disable is treated as a config mutation under audit_exports.write. Policy bundle does not define any readable secret fields; all secrets are references.

Kevin Osei (comment, 2025-03-24): Add a short note in docs that retention is separate from export and that exports are best-effort with retry (at-least-once). We get SOC2 questionnaire confusion here.
Author (reply): Added doc note pointing to retention policy language (no net-new claims). Avoided over-specifying delivery semantics beyond at-least-once.

Benji Okafor (approval, 2025-03-26): Looks good; matches endpoint permission checks in redwood PR. +1.

CI
- policy-bundle / validate: PASS
- policy-bundle / lint: PASS
- policy-bundle / unit-tests: PASS
- release-dry-run: PASS

Merge
Squash merged to main on 2025-03-26.

Release notes
Enterprise: Introduces granular RBAC permissions for managing SIEM-ready audit export configurations and viewing exporter health/status (beta).

- Secrets are represented as references (vault path / secret manager ID) and never exposed as readable fields via RBAC in this bundle.
- audit_exports.write does not imply audit event read access.
- audit_exports.status.read is separated so auditors/SecOps can monitor health without config write privileges.

2) Default role mapping updates
- enterprise_admin (and legacy admin-equivalent roles)
  - Granted: audit_exports.read, audit_exports.write, audit_exports.secrets.rotate, audit_exports.status.read
- security_auditor
  - Granted: audit_exports.read, audit_exports.status.read
  - Not granted: audit_exports.write, audit_exports.secrets.rotate
- readonly / billing / developer roles
  - No change (no export management permissions added)

3) Policy docs / examples
- Added a short section to the role catalog describing audit export permissions and recommended assignment ("Admin owns configuration; Auditor owns monitoring + evidence"), plus a note that export delivery is at-least-once and downstream dedup is recommended.

Why this approach
- Matches separation-of-duties expectations from Security reviews (config vs oversight).
- Minimizes blast radius if an auditor token is compromised (can’t change destinations/filters or rotate creds).
- Keeps permission granularity aligned with control plane endpoint grouping.

Testing
- bundle lint/validate
- role compilation tests
- snapshot update for default roles

Rollout
- No runtime change; this is a policy bundle update.
- Control plane enforcement remains feature-flagged per tenant allowlist.

Follow-ups (not in this PR)
- Add a dedicated "security_ops" role variant if we see customers wanting status.read without read of config details (currently status.read still requires config metadata for Console UX).
- Revisit permission naming consistency with other integrations (e.g., destinations.*) once API surface stabilizes.

- [x] New permissions documented
- [x] Default role mappings updated
- [x] CI green
- [x] Reviewed with Security + Control Plane

Review notes / discussion
Ethan Park (requested changes, 2025-03-22): Can we split status read from config read? Console status page is going to call both endpoints; but conceptually we should keep them separate.
Author (reply): Added audit_exports.status.read as separate permission. Kept audit_exports.read required for config listing/details. status.read is now separate and can be granted independently. (Control plane already checks both as needed.)

Marcus Lin (requested changes, 2025-03-23): Please ensure auditors cannot rotate credentials and cannot enable/disable exports; also confirm no secret material is in any readable resource fields.
Author (reply): Confirmed. Rotation is gated by audit_exports.secrets.rotate. Enable/disable is treated as a config mutation under audit_exports.write. Policy bundle does not define any readable secret fields; all secrets are references.

Author (reply): Added doc note pointing to retention policy language (no net-new claims). Avoided over-specifying delivery semantics beyond at-least-once.

Benji Okafor (approval, 2025-03-26): Looks good; matches endpoint permission checks in redwood PR. +1.

- policy-bundle / lint: PASS
- policy-bundle / unit-tests: PASS
- release-dry-run: PASS

Merge
Squash merged to main on 2025-03-26.

title: Integrate Smart Routing with burst admission decisions (fallback + backpressure)
preview: Motivation: Dedicated burst capacity requires routing to consult the Burst Admission Controller before borrowing from shared burst pools, and to behave safely when burst is denied (protecting non-burst tenants + preserving determinism). This PR wires the routing/gateway path into the admission controller, adds deterministic policy evaluation inputs, and defines the fallback/backpressure behaviors for denied/timeout cases. Summary of behavior: For Dedicated tenants with burst enabled, routing computes a BurstContext (tenant_id, region, hw_class, baseline allocation id, requested_concurrency/requested_tokens estimate, priority tier) and calls the Burst Admission Controller synchronously at request admission time. If admitted, routing tags the request with the admission grant_id and burst_pool_id and routes to the burst pool endpoint group; if denied, routing falls back to baseline dedicated capacity when available (no pool borrowing) and otherwise applies backpressure (HTTP 429 with structured denial reason + Retry-After) rather than retrying across neighbors. Determinism: The admission request includes a stable request_key (derived from gateway request_id + model_route + tenant_id) and a policy_snapshot_hash from the control-plane config cache. Routing does not “shop around” across pools/regions/hardware classes. For the initial release, burst is strictly within-region and within hardware class. Failure modes: If the admission controller call times out or returns 5xx, routing treats it as a deny with reason=admission_unavailable and routes only to baseline dedicated capacity; if baseline is saturated, we backpressure with 503 (no burst) to avoid accidental neighbor impact. Observability: Adds routing-side counters and latency histograms for admission calls, denials by reason, and fallback usage, and propagates denial reason codes to the client error surface (aligned with pr-23795). Notes: This PR intentionally does not implement queueing-on-deny; the chosen semantics for v1 are immediate decision + client-driven retry using Retry-After guidance. Queueing can be revisited once we have stronger end-to-end SLO evidence and per-tenant buffering policies.

title: Add anchorwise fidelity audit and CI explainability hook
preview:
Motivation: Teams running long-lived prompt sets have asked for more granular, explainable regression signals tied to 'anchors' (representative examples) so that CI failures are actionable without manual triage. This PR introduces an anchorwise fidelity audit that computes per-anchor fidelity and saliency deltas, surfaces interpretable failure reasons (e.g., token shift, hallucinated span, idempotency drop), and wires a CI hook that annotates PRs with a compact explainability report.

What this changes: adds a lightweight audit runner (hooks/anchorwise_audit.py) that: (1) replays the prompt-set against a reference baseline and the candidate change, (2) computes per-anchor metrics (fidelity_score, token_saliency_delta, semantic_overlap_pct), (3) runs a stacked heuristic classifier to attribute probable failure cause, and (4) emits a normalized JSON+CSV summary consumed by the CI hook. The CI hook (ci/hooks/anchor_explainability_gate.yml) runs in the eval pipeline and will soft-fail on degradations exceeding configured thresholds and post an explainability comment to the PR with the top 5 anchors requiring attention.

Checklist (implementation & rollout):
- [x] Core audit runner implementation with unit tests
- [x] CI hook definition and comment poster integration
- [x] Documentation: docs/anchorwise_fidelity.md with metric definitions and remediation guidance
- [x] Backwards-compatible config: default thresholds set to conservative values and overridable via .evalrc in repo
- [x] Smoke run against recent canary commits to validate signal and false-positive rate
- [x] Telemetry hooks for false-positive collection and opt-out flag

Testing & validation: ran unit tests locally and in CI; performed a 48-hour shadow run on the redwood/main prompt set (shadow run id: shadow-20250318-02) to confirm the anchor ranking aligns with human triage in >85% of sampled cases. Thresholds were tuned to target ~5% soft-fail rate on historical diffs.

Rollout plan: merge to main with feature flagged disabled by default. Flip feature flag for eval-maintainers and run as hard gate for a single team for two weeks before organization-wide opt-in.

Risks and mitigations: heuristics can be noisy for very small anchors (<= 3 tokens); we filter those out and include a warning in the audit output. The CI hook is soft-fail by default to avoid blocking regressions in flight.

title: Burst: standardize denial reason codes + surface to routing/gateway
preview: Motivation: During burst beta we’ve had multiple cases where customers/Support see generic 429/overload errors without actionable context (e.g., cap reached vs fleet safety vs cooldown). This PR standardizes a small, stable set of burst denial reason codes in the admission controller response and propagates them through routing -> gateway so we can (a) render clear customer-facing error messages, (b) expose denial breakdowns in Console and dashboards, and (c) reduce Support back-and-forth by making the denial cause inspectable. Summary of changes: 1) Introduce a versioned enum `BurstDenialReason` in `common/capacity` shared types with explicit semantics and non-overlapping categories. 2) Admission controller returns `decision=DENY` with `reason_code` and optional `retry_after_ms` for retryable denials (e.g., COOLDOWN_ACTIVE / POOL_SATURATED). 3) Routing stores the reason code in request context and includes it in structured logs + trace attributes; for HTTP responses we map denial to a consistent error payload and set `x-redwood-burst-denial-reason` header for Dedicated tenants (guarded behind feature flag). 4) Gateway maps denial reason codes to a stable `error.code` string (e.g., `burst_denied_pool_saturated`) and preserves existing status codes (429 for capacity-style denials, 503 for internal admission errors/timeouts) to avoid breaking clients. 5) Add Prometheus counter `redwood_burst_denials_total{reason=...}` with strict label set; no tenant_id label to avoid cardinality blowups. 6) Tests: unit tests for mapping table, golden tests for error payloads, and an integration test that asserts determinism: same inputs -> same denial code. Backwards compatibility: Existing clients that only look at status code/message are unaffected. Header + structured field are additive and behind `burst.denial_reasons.enabled` flag default OFF until rollout. Reason code set (initial): `TENANT_BURST_DISABLED`, `TENANT_CAP_REACHED`, `MAX_BURST_RATIO_EXCEEDED`, `COOLDOWN_ACTIVE`, `POOL_SATURATED`, `FLEET_SAFETY_CAP`, `TELEMETRY_STALE`, `POLICY_EVAL_ERROR`, `ADMISSION_TIMEOUT`. Follow-ups: Console surface (ENG-4855) will use the new read API + denial breakdown; Support playbook update in ENG-4881 to align messaging and recommended retry/backoff per reason.

title: Propagate route + model version context into runtime cache metrics (with cardinality allowlists)
preview:
## Context
We’re landing the cache telemetry suite (hit/miss, reuse depth, evictions, wasted prefill). For the signals to be actionable in dashboards and in the recommendations MVP, we need consistent slicing keys that match how Redwood operates: route, model, model version, deployment mode, and region.

This PR wires request-scoped context from the gateway/control plane into the runtime metrics emitter and applies strict allowlists/coercion so we don’t accidentally create per-tenant/per-request label explosions.

Related: ENG-4823 (route/model context joinability), ENG-4821 (metric defs + tags).

## What this changes
- Adds `TelemetryContext` (derived from `RequestContext`) to runtime metric emission sites for cache metrics.
- Adds the following metric labels (when enabled):
  - `rw_route`: stable route identifier (normalized, allowlisted; NOT tenant-specific)
  - `rw_model`: model identifier (allowlisted)
  - `rw_model_version`: model version identifier (allowlisted, stable across regions)
  - `rw_deployment_mode`: `hosted|dedicated|private`
  - `rw_region`: coarse region (existing tag in some metrics; now consistent for cache metrics)
- Enforces allowlists + coercion:
  - If a value is missing or not in the allowlist, we emit `other` and increment a debug counter.
  - No tenant IDs, no project IDs, no raw route paths, no prompt hashes.
- Adds debug/health counters:
  - `rw_runtime_telemetry_label_coercions_total{label_name=..., reason=...}`
  - `rw_runtime_telemetry_context_missing_total{field=...}`
  These are intended for internal validation only and are not broken down by route/model.

## Cardinality policy (implementation details)
The runtime receives context values from the gateway as already-normalized strings (e.g., `route_key`, `model_key`, `model_version_id`). Runtime does not attempt to “compute” route keys from paths.

Allowlist sources:
- Static allowlist shipped via config (initial rollout)
- Optional dynamic allowlist refresh (follow-up; stubbed interface included)

Coercion behavior:
- Empty/unknown values → `other`
- Over-length values (defense-in-depth) → `other` with reason `too_long`

Example:
- `rw_route = chat_support_prod` (allowlisted)
- `rw_route = /v1/chat/completions?customer=foo` (NOT allowed; would be coerced)

## Flags
- `telemetry.cache_metrics.enable_context_labels` (default: true in staging/canary, false in prod until rollout gate)
- `telemetry.cache_metrics.route_allowlist_path`
- `telemetry.cache_metrics.model_allowlist_path`
- `telemetry.cache_metrics.model_version_allowlist_path`

## Tests
- Unit tests for allowlist + coercion reasons.
- Regression test asserting that when context labels are disabled, the cache metric label set remains unchanged.
- Integration-level handler tests validate that context is plumbed for chat/generate/embeddings/rerank.

## Notes / Follow-ups
- Ingestion-side guards still apply (observability-pack PR adds drop rules for forbidden label combos); runtime coercion is defense-in-depth.
- Next PR will turn on `enable_context_labels` in prod behind the rollout checklist once allowlists are populated for top routes/models.

## Checklist
- [x] No raw prompts / no request IDs added to labels
- [x] Label values are allowlisted + bounded
- [x] Added internal counters to validate coercion rates
- [x] Added tests
- [x] Updated internal docs for join keys

---
### Implementation snippet (illustrative)
`cache_metrics.cc` now takes a `TelemetryContext` and merges it into the label set:

```cc
MetricLabels labels = base_labels;
if (flags.enable_context_labels) {
  labels["rw_route"] = allowlists.route.CoerceOrOther(ctx.route_key);
  labels["rw_model"] = allowlists.model.CoerceOrOther(ctx.model_key);
  labels["rw_model_version"] = allowlists.model_version.CoerceOrOther(ctx.model_version_id);
  labels["rw_deployment_mode"] = DeploymentModeToString(ctx.deployment_mode);
  labels["rw_region"] = ctx.region.empty() ? "other" : ctx.region;
}
metrics::Counter("rw_runtime_cache_kv_hits_total", labels).Increment(hits);
```

---
## Review conversation (highlights)
**Omar El-Sayed (Runtime):** “Make sure we don’t accidentally tag retries with different context; the labels must be stable across the request lifecycle.”

**Ethan Park (author):** “Yep — context is attached to the top-level RequestContext and copied into the per-attempt telemetry context. We also keep the label injection in one place (metrics emitter) so retries don’t ‘drift’ based on handler code paths.”

**Nadia Rahman (Telemetry schema):** “Can we keep the label names aligned with the naming policy (`rw_route`, `rw_model`, `rw_model_version`)? Also prefer `deployment_mode` values to be a fixed enum.”

**Ethan Park:** “Updated to `rw_*` label names and hard-coded enum strings (`hosted|dedicated|private`). Added a unit test to prevent new deployment mode strings without updating the allowlist policy doc.”

**Caleb Johnson (Cardinality controls):** “I’m worried about `rw_region` exploding via AZ or private cluster names. Please bound it (coarse region only) or coerce unknowns.”

**Ethan Park:** “Good catch. `rw_region` comes from the existing region enum we use in gateway metrics (e.g., `us-east-1`, `eu-west-1`). Added a length guard + `other` fallback, and a `context_missing_total{field=region}` counter.”

**Noah Patel (Runtime architecture):** “Do we need `rw_model_version` vs `rw_model_revision`? Consistency matters for joins.”

**Ethan Park:** “Using `model_version_id` per the control-plane contract and Slack thread on joinability. Added `platform/contracts/telemetry_context.md` with examples and a note that ‘version’ is the stable join key.”

Approvals:
- Omar El-Sayed: ✅ Approved
- Nadia Rahman: ✅ Approved (after label rename)
- Caleb Johnson: ✅ Approved (after region bounding + coercion counter)
- Logan Wright: ✅ Approved

---
## CI
- buildkite/runtime-linux: PASS
- buildkite/runtime-tests: PASS
- buildkite/lint: PASS
- sanitizer-smoke: PASS

Merged via squash to `main`.

title: prioritized-rca-scoring-and-playbook-recommender
preview:
Motivation: on-call responders are overwhelmed by alert noise and often run a suite of irrelevant playbooks. This PR implements a lightweight root-cause scoring layer + playbook prioritizer that surfaces the most likely mitigations.

What it does (single-pass summary):
- Adds a signal fusion module that scores candidate root causes from alert metadata, recent SLO burn-rate windows (via slo-toolkit hooks), and recent deploy/rollout activity.
- Introduces a playbook prioritizer that ranks runbooks by expected mitigation probability and time-to-mitigate (TTM).
- Adds a new /incident suggest-playbook command for Slack that returns 1-3 prioritized actions and confidence bands.
- Exposes an opt-in per-service configuration to tune weights and to pin preferred playbooks.
- Includes metrics and tracing for the scoring decisions to aid audits and regression debugging.

Why this is different from previous runbook helpers: the prioritizer is probabilistic and time-aware (uses historic mitigation durations) rather than a static tag-matching engine. It is intentionally lightweight (no heavy ML infra) so it can run in the incident-bot process and be used in private deployments.

Motivation: on-call responders are overwhelmed by alert noise and often run a suite of irrelevant playbooks. This PR implements a lightweight root-cause scoring layer + playbook prioritizer that surfaces the most likely mitigations.

What it does (single-pass summary):
- Adds a signal fusion module that scores candidate root causes from alert metadata, recent SLO burn-rate windows (via slo-toolkit hooks), and recent deploy/rollout activity.
- Introduces a playbook prioritizer that ranks runbooks by expected mitigation probability and time-to-mitigate (TTM).
- Adds a new /incident suggest-playbook command for Slack that returns 1-3 prioritized actions and confidence bands.
- Exposes an opt-in per-service configuration to tune weights and to pin preferred playbooks.
- Includes metrics and tracing for the scoring decisions to aid audits and regression debugging.

Why this is different from previous runbook helpers: the prioritizer is probabilistic and time-aware (uses historic mitigation durations) rather than a static tag-matching engine. It is intentionally lightweight (no heavy ML infra) so it can run in the incident-bot process and be used in private deployments.

Compatibility notes: integrates with existing slo-toolkit event API (v2) and incident-bot's existing alert enrichment pipeline. Backwards compatible: if slo-toolkit data is not present the scorer gracefully degrades to metadata-only heuristics.

Testing and rollout: feature guarded behind a runtime flag; telemetry opt-in enforced per-organization. Can be enabled for a single team for staged rollout.
